program.name: SUSTAINABLE COMPETITIVENESS IN TOURISM - Supporting Tourism SMEs to become more competitive as they transform into more sustainable and resilient
program.call_summary:
Call Summary — SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME
Identificación. Convocatoria SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME-01, titulada "Sustainable Competitiveness in Tourism — Supporting Tourism SMEs to become more competitive as they transform into more sustainable and resilient", enmarcada en el Single Market Programme (SMP) bajo el pilar COSME (Competitiveness of Enterprises and SMEs). Gestionada por la European Innovation Council and SMEs Executive Agency (EISMEA). Versión vigente: V2.0 del 26 de abril de 2026 (Corrigendum). Tipo de acción: Action grant en modalidad budget-based mixed actual cost.
Plazos clave. La convocatoria se abrió el 3 de marzo de 2026. El plazo de presentación cierra el 20 de mayo de 2026 a las 17:00 CET (hora de Bruselas). La evaluación por EISMEA tendrá lugar entre junio y julio de 2026, con comunicación de resultados entre agosto y septiembre. La firma del Grant Agreement se prevé entre octubre y noviembre de 2026, y el inicio efectivo de los proyectos seleccionados se sitúa estimativamente entre noviembre y diciembre de 2026.
Presupuesto. El presupuesto total de la convocatoria es de 6.900.000 €, ampliable hasta un 20% adicional (8.280.000 €) para financiar una propuesta extra si quedara disponibilidad presupuestaria. Se espera financiar alrededor de cinco proyectos. Cada propuesta debe situarse en un rango de subvención solicitada de 1.200.000 € a 1.400.000 €. La tasa de cofinanciación europea es del 90% para los costes generales del proyecto y del 100% para el apoyo financiero a terceros (FSTP). Se abona un prefinanciamiento del 70% del importe máximo, un pago intermedio en la mitad del proyecto y el pago de saldo al cierre tras el informe final.
Objetivo central. Financiar el establecimiento y consolidación de rutas turísticas temáticas y/o geográficas sostenibles, así como el fortalecimiento de los ecosistemas de pymes asociados. Las rutas deben quedar plenamente operativas y auto-sostenibles al cierre del cofinanciamiento europeo.
Prioridades temáticas (scope). La convocatoria prioriza la creación de rutas temáticas y/o geográficas que movilicen el ecosistema turístico; la corrección simultánea del over-tourism en destinos saturados y del under-tourism en áreas menos visitadas; la diversificación de la oferta turística para abordar la estacionalidad; el fomento del turismo regenerativo y sostenible que respete la autenticidad local; la promoción del turismo temático y de intereses especiales (cultural, gastronómico, deportivo, naturaleza, patrimonio tangible e intangible); el bienestar y desarrollo sostenible de las comunidades locales; la transición gemela verde y digital (twin transition) de las pymes turísticas; y el desarrollo territorial equilibrado en regiones rurales, remotas, costeras y menos desarrolladas.
Composición obligatoria del consorcio. Entre 5 y 8 entidades independientes. Mínimo 3 organizaciones de gestión de destinos (DMOs) procedentes de al menos 2 países elegibles diferentes. Mínimo 2 organizaciones de apoyo empresarial (BSOs) procedentes de al menos 2 países elegibles diferentes. Las DMOs son entidades públicas o privadas que gestionan estrategias turísticas locales, regionales o nacionales. Las BSOs son incubadoras, aceleradoras, hubs de innovación, universidades, centros de investigación o cualquier organización con capacidad probada de apoyo empresarial a pymes. Todas las entidades deben validar su PIC en el Participant Register antes de la firma del Grant Agreement.
Duración del proyecto. Entre 30 y 48 meses. Las extensiones son posibles si están debidamente justificadas mediante enmienda al Grant Agreement.
Estructura obligatoria de paquetes de trabajo. Cuatro paquetes de trabajo son obligatorios. WP1: establecimiento o rediseño de la ruta y del ecosistema, con definición del trazado, mapeo de stakeholders, gobernanza, KPIs y plan de negocio. WP2: desarrollo y engagement del ecosistema, subdividido en WP2.1 (engagement y capacity building sin financiación directa) y WP2.2 (Financial Support to Third Parties — apoyo financiero directo a pymes vía cascade funding). WP3: comunicación, promoción y branding del ecosistema de la ruta, con plataforma digital obligatoria, dos vídeos institucionales, participación en evento presencial obligatorio en Bruselas (2 días, 5 personas del consorcio) y alineamiento con Destination Europe framework. WP4: producción de buenas prácticas y casos de estudio, con publicación obligatoria de resultados en la EU Tourism Platform.
Esquema FSTP (cascade funding). Obligatorio en el WP2.2. Convocatoria pública, abierta, transparente y competitiva, con un mínimo de dos meses de apertura. Se seleccionan entre 10 y 30 pymes mediante criterios objetivos publicados. Importes en formato lump sum entre 7.000 € y 15.000 € por pyme, con posibilidad de tramos diferenciados. Las pymes elegibles incluyen tanto sectores tradicionales (turismo, gastronomía, cultura, deporte, cadenas de suministro locales, construcción y patrimonio) como proveedores de soluciones digitales (TravelTech, plataformas, AR, sistemas data-driven). Los proyectos financiados deben contribuir a establecer, fortalecer o actualizar la ruta y su oferta conjunta, asegurar su sostenibilidad ambiental, económica y organizacional, mejorar visibilidad y branding, diversificar la oferta, o fomentar la cooperación intersectorial.
Criterios de evaluación. Cuatro criterios con puntuación ponderada sobre 100. Relevance (30 puntos, umbral 16): claridad y consistencia del proyecto, alineamiento con scope y prioridades de la convocatoria, dimensión transnacional, replicabilidad y potencial de cooperación transfronteriza. Quality – Project Design and Implementation (30 puntos, umbral 16): calidad técnica, diseño de la ruta y del ecosistema, metodología, identificación de riesgos, monitorización y coste-eficiencia. Quality – Project Team and Cooperation Arrangements (30 puntos, umbral 16): calidad del consorcio, configuración del equipo y mecanismos de cooperación. Impact (10 puntos, umbral 6): ambición e impacto esperado a largo plazo, número de pymes apoyadas, estrategia de diseminación, sostenibilidad ambiental y económica de la ruta tras el cierre del cofinanciamiento. Umbral global de aprobación: 70 puntos sobre 100.
Procedimiento de evaluación. Procedimiento estándar de presentación y evaluación en una sola fase. Las propuestas son revisadas por un comité de evaluación asistido por expertos externos independientes. Primero se verifica admisibilidad y elegibilidad, después se evalúan según criterios de capacidad operativa y award criteria. Las propuestas se ordenan por puntuación. En caso de empate, se prioriza por temática no cubierta, por puntuación de Relevance, después de Impact, después de Quality, y finalmente por equilibrio geográfico y temático del portfolio.
Impacto esperado. La Comisión espera de los proyectos financiados: refuerzo de la competitividad sostenible del ecosistema turístico europeo; mayor competitividad de las pymes turísticas mediante innovación, calidad, digitalización y desarrollo de ofertas de alto valor; mejora de la resiliencia frente a desafíos económicos, ambientales y sociales; mayor adopción de prácticas verdes, tecnologías nuevas, soluciones data-driven y enfoques circulares; crecimiento económico y creación de empleo en regiones rurales, remotas, costeras y menos desarrolladas; promoción de modelos de negocio sostenibles e inclusivos; desarrollo territorial equilibrado entre destinos saturados y áreas menos visitadas; avance de la twin transition; cooperación transfronteriza e intersectorial reforzada; y producción de buenas prácticas replicables.
Marco estratégico de referencia. Las propuestas deben demostrar alineamiento con el Transition Pathway for Tourism, la European Agenda for Tourism 2030, el European Green Deal, el Circular Economy Action Plan, la Farm to Fork Strategy, la EU Biodiversity Strategy 2030, el Digital Compass 2030, el Pact for Skills (Tourism partnership), PANTOUR, el European Accessibility Act, la Sustainable and Smart Mobility Strategy, la Sustainable Blue Economy y el Destination Europe framework. Los KPIs deberán ser compatibles con el EU Tourism Dashboard. Los resultados deberán publicarse obligatoriamente en la EU Tourism Platform.
Obligaciones y restricciones especiales. Una entidad solo puede participar en una propuesta bajo esta convocatoria. Prohibición estricta de doble financiación. Cumplimiento de las EU restrictive measures y EU conditionality measures. Las pymes financiadas vía FSTP no pueden ser presentadas como recibiendo financiación directa de la UE, sino como "co-funded by the EU". Obligación de participar en evento presencial en Bruselas convocado por la Comisión (2 días, 5 personas del consorcio). Obligación de producir al menos dos vídeos cortos de 1-2 minutos cada uno. Obligación de publicar resultados en la EU Tourism Platform. Reporte trimestral y anual a EISMEA. Firma de un Consortium Agreement interno entre los socios. Las propuestas se presentan exclusivamente a través del EU Funding & Tenders Portal Electronic Submission System.

eligible_entity_types.label: BSO (Business Support Organisation — apoyo a empresas)

eligibility.additional_rules:
Composición específica del consorcio. Además del mínimo y máximo general de socios, hay dos sub-requisitos acumulativos: el consorcio debe incluir un mínimo de 3 organizaciones de gestión de destinos (DMOs) procedentes de al menos 2 países elegibles diferentes, y un mínimo de 2 organizaciones de apoyo empresarial (BSOs) procedentes de al menos 2 países elegibles diferentes. Las DMOs se definen como entidades públicas o privadas que gestionan estrategias turísticas locales, regionales o nacionales. Las BSOs incluyen incubadoras, aceleradoras, hubs de innovación, universidades, centros de investigación y otras organizaciones con capacidad probada de apoyo empresarial a pymes. Los entes de la UE (excepto el Joint Research Centre) no pueden formar parte del consorcio. Las personas físicas no son elegibles, salvo autónomos sin personalidad jurídica separada.
Una sola propuesta por entidad. Un mismo aplicante (beneficiario o entidad afiliada) solo puede presentar una propuesta bajo esta convocatoria y solo puede recibir una financiación. No se permite participar en varias propuestas simultáneamente. Si una entidad participa en varias, solo una será aceptada y evaluada, y se exigirá la retirada de las demás (o serán rechazadas directamente).
Localización de las actividades. Las actividades del proyecto deben tener lugar exclusivamente en los países elegibles (Estados miembros de la UE y países asociados al programa SMP).
Duración del proyecto. Entre 30 meses como mínimo y 48 meses como máximo. Las extensiones posteriores son posibles solo si están debidamente justificadas y se tramitan mediante enmienda formal al Grant Agreement.
Rango presupuestario por propuesta. La subvención solicitada debe situarse entre 1.200.000 € y 1.400.000 €. Se admiten propuestas con otros importes, pero el rango oficial marca la expectativa de la Comisión. La subvención concedida puede ser inferior a la solicitada.
Estructura obligatoria de paquetes de trabajo. El proyecto debe articularse en cuatro paquetes de trabajo obligatorios: WP1 (establecimiento o rediseño de la ruta y del ecosistema), WP2 (desarrollo y engagement del ecosistema, incluyendo obligatoriamente el FSTP), WP3 (comunicación, promoción y branding), y WP4 (producción de buenas prácticas y casos de estudio para replicabilidad). Cada WP tiene actividades mínimas y deliverables específicos.
Financial Support to Third Parties (FSTP) obligatorio. El esquema de apoyo financiero directo a pymes es obligatorio. Debe articularse mediante convocatoria pública, abierta, transparente y competitiva, con un mínimo de dos meses de apertura. Se deben seleccionar entre 10 y 30 pymes mediante criterios objetivos publicados. Los importes individuales deben situarse entre 7.000 € y 15.000 € por pyme, en formato lump sum, con posibilidad de tramos diferenciados. Las pymes financiadas no pueden presentarse como receptoras directas de fondos UE, sino como "co-funded by the EU". Las pymes deben firmar declaración de doble financiación al inicio y al cierre.
Tasas de cofinanciación diferenciadas. El cofinanciamiento europeo es del 100% para los costes de FSTP (categoría D.1) y del 90% para todas las demás categorías de coste. El consorcio debe aportar el 10% de cofinanciación sobre las categorías generales.
Obligaciones específicas de comunicación. Producción obligatoria de al menos dos vídeos cortos (1 a 2 minutos cada uno, uno al inicio y otro hacia el cierre del proyecto). Participación obligatoria en un evento presencial de dos días en Bruselas convocado por EISMEA o la Comisión Europea, con cinco representantes del consorcio. La estrategia de comunicación debe alinearse con el Destination Europe framework y sincronizarse con Visit Europe portal y los portales nacionales de promoción turística. La medición debe usar los EC Comm Indicators.
Publicación obligatoria de resultados. Los resultados, las buenas prácticas y los materiales del WP4 deben publicarse obligatoriamente en la EU Tourism Platform. Adicionalmente, los socios deben publicar los resultados en sus propias webs.
Cumplimiento del marco político europeo. Las propuestas deben cumplir con los intereses y prioridades de la UE (medio ambiente, política social, seguridad, política industrial y comercial). Deben respetar los valores europeos y la política de la Comisión en materia reputacional. Deben tomar en cuenta los resultados de otros proyectos financiados por programas UE y describir las complementariedades en el Part B del formulario de aplicación. Deben demostrar alineamiento con el Transition Pathway for Tourism y la European Agenda for Tourism 2030.
Capacidad financiera y operacional. Los aplicantes deben demostrar recursos estables y suficientes para implementar el proyecto y aportar su cofinanciación. La verificación de capacidad financiera se aplica a todos los beneficiarios excepto entes públicos, organizaciones internacionales y entidades con subvención individual inferior a 60.000 €. Se exigirá documentación contable (cuenta de pérdidas y ganancias, balance, plan de negocio, informe de auditoría) durante la fase de preparación del Grant Agreement.
Validación PIC obligatoria. Todos los beneficiarios y entidades afiliadas deben registrarse en el Participant Register de la Comisión y obtener validación por la Central Validation Service (REA Validation) antes de la presentación de la propuesta. Se requiere documentación que acredite el estatus legal y origen de la entidad.
Sujeción al Model Grant Agreement. Todas las obligaciones técnicas, financieras y administrativas se regirán por el Annotated Grant Agreement (AGA) de la Comisión en su versión vigente. Esto incluye reglas sobre cost eligibility, reporting periods, payments, audits, conflict of interest, IPR, ética, protección de datos personales (GDPR), y obligaciones de archivo documental por un mínimo de cinco años tras el pago de saldo.
Restricciones específicas UE. Las entidades sujetas a EU restrictive measures (Artículos 29 TEU y 215 TFEU) no son elegibles en ninguna capacidad (beneficiario, afiliado, partner asociado, subcontratista o receptor de FSTP). Las entidades sujetas a EU conditionality measures (Reglamento 2020/2092) tampoco son elegibles. Actualmente esto afecta de forma específica a los trusts húngaros de interés público establecidos bajo el Acto IX/2021.
Prohibición de doble financiación. Las actividades financiadas por esta convocatoria no pueden recibir simultáneamente otra subvención de fondos UE (incluyendo fondos UE gestionados por autoridades nacionales, fondos regionales, fondos agrícolas, etc.), salvo en el marco de EU Synergies actions formalmente reconocidas.
Reglas sobre subcontratación. La subcontratación está permitida pero limitada. Las tareas centrales del proyecto (core tasks) deben ser ejecutadas por los propios beneficiarios o entidades afiliadas, no subcontratadas. La subcontratación debe estar prevista en el presupuesto inicial o aprobada por modificación.
Modalidad de presentación. Presentación exclusivamente electrónica a través del EU Funding & Tenders Portal Electronic Submission System. No se aceptan presentaciones en papel. Los documentos obligatorios incluyen el Application Form Part A (información administrativa), Application Form Part B (descripción técnica del proyecto), Detailed Budget Table en formato Excel, y los anexos requeridos. Procedimiento de evaluación en una sola fase (one-stage submission + one-step evaluation).
Umbrales de evaluación. La propuesta debe superar los umbrales mínimos individuales en los cuatro criterios de evaluación (16/30 en Relevance, 16/30 en Quality - Design, 16/30 en Quality - Team, 6/10 en Impact) y el umbral global de 70 puntos sobre 100 para ser considerada financiable.
Obligación de informe (survey). Los beneficiarios y receptores de FSTP están obligados a cumplimentar la encuesta de seguimiento del programa SMP, que alimenta la monitorización y evaluación de la convocatoria y la programación futura. Los receptores de FSTP pueden cumplimentar la encuesta en su idioma nacional.
eligibility.writing_style: All answers must be written in clear, structured paragraphs (never bullet points) using the COSME register systematically. The vocabulary must include: twin transition, competitiveness, digitalisation, resilience, tourism ecosystem, route ecosystem, stakeholder mapping, over-tourism vs under-tourism, regenerative tourism, balanced territorial development, intangible cultural heritage, value chain integration, and cross-border cooperation. The structuring concept of every answer is a transnational tourism route (thematic and/or geographical) and its ecosystem of SMEs — not generic SME support. The narrative tone is institutional, evidence-based and policy-aware: every problem statement must be framed at European level (not only at the project's territorial scope), supported by data from EU sources (European Commission, Eurostat tourism statistics, EU Tourism Dashboard, OECD Tourism, UNWTO, Transition Pathway for Tourism), and explicitly linked to the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME objectives (route creation, over/under-tourism rebalancing, diversification, regenerative tourism, intangible heritage valorisation, twin transition acceleration, balanced territorial development). Each answer should follow a strict logical sequence: structural problem → evidence → urgency → rationale for intervention → call alignment → European added value. Indicators must always include unit of measurement, baseline value, and target value, and must be compatible with EU Tourism Dashboard categories (Economic, Environmental, Social, Digital, Governance) so that results can be aggregated at EU level. Programme framing must consistently reference SMP-COSME (Single Market Programme — Competitiveness of Enterprises and SMEs), managed by EISMEA, under the SMP Annotated Grant Agreement (AGA). Reference EU policy frameworks operationally (not nominally): European Agenda for Tourism 2030, Transition Pathway for Tourism, European Green Deal, Circular Economy Action Plan, Farm to Fork, EU Biodiversity Strategy 2030, Digital Compass 2030, Pact for Skills (Tourism) and PANTOUR, European Accessibility Act, Sustainable and Smart Mobility Strategy, Sustainable Blue Economy, Destination Europe framework. Tone should be confident, concrete and grounded; avoid abstract claims; always articulate what is new versus what is built upon; always quantify where measurable; always describe mechanisms (not principles).
eligibility.ai_detection_rules: ABSOLUTE PROHIBITIONS. Never reference Erasmus+ or EACEA — this is SMP-COSME managed by EISMEA under the SMP Annotated Grant Agreement (AGA). Never use the Erasmus+ logo on any material — only the 'Funded by the European Union' emblem with the standard disclaimer ('Views and opinions expressed are however those of the author(s) only and do not necessarily reflect those of the European Union or the European Innovation Council and SMEs Executive Agency (EISMEA). Neither the European Union nor the granting authority can be held responsible for them.'). Never present FSTP recipients as receiving direct EU funding — they are 'co-funded by the European Union'. Never describe tourism SMEs as consortium partners — they are FSTP recipients only. Never propose FSTP lump sums outside the 7,000-15,000 € band, or fewer than 10 / more than 30 SMEs supported. Never omit conflict of interest declarations or double-funding declarations from the FSTP scheme. Never create a 5th WP for coordination — the call imposes 4 mandatory thematic WPs (WP1 Route establishment, WP2 Ecosystem development with FSTP, WP3 Communication & branding, WP4 Best practices & replicability); coordination is transversal. Never imply double funding from EU sources for the same activities. Never breach GDPR, EU restrictive measures, or SMP-AGA audit obligations (5-year retention post-final payment). MANDATORY FRAMINGS. Always frame the problem at European level. Always reference SMP / EISMEA / SMP-AGA when citing legal or programmatic context. Always confirm consortium compliance (minimum 3 DMOs + 2 BSOs in 2+ countries each, total 5-8 entities). Always align KPIs with EU Tourism Dashboard categories. Always include the 2 mandatory short videos (1-2 min), the mandatory Brussels event (2 days, 5 people) and publication on the EU Tourism Platform. Always reference the Destination Europe framework, Visit Europe portal and national portals (visit France, Spain.info) where dissemination is described. Always cite concrete previous projects when claiming continuity (no abstract 'building on previous experience'). Always rate risk likelihood and impact AFTER mitigation. Always describe a post-project legal entity (EGTC / AEIE / transnational not-for-profit association) when discussing sustainability — never claim sustainability without operational mechanisms (legal, financial, organisational). Always use EC Communication Indicators when reporting dissemination reach.

sections.title: 4. WORK PLAN, WORK PACKAGES, ACTIVITIES, RESOURCES AND TIMING

questions.title: Impact and ambition
questions.description:
Please address each guiding point presented in the Call document/Programme Guide under the award criterion Impact.

Define the expected short, medium and long-term effects of the project. Who are the target groups? How will the target groups benefit concretely from the project and what would change for them?
questions.general_context: Target groups for this call typically include: (a) primary — tourism SMEs along the route (FSTP recipients); (b) consortium partner organisations (DMOs, BSOs) that strengthen their capacity; (c) wider tourism ecosystem stakeholders (local authorities, parks, cultural associations, intangible heritage carriers, mobility operators, tour operators); (d) indirect — local communities of the route territories; (e) European-level — other DMOs, BSOs and tourism networks that will replicate the route model; (f) policy level — EISMEA, DG GROW, national tourism authorities. The impact must be articulated in three temporal layers: short-term (during and immediately after activities — awareness, skills, FSTP funding deployed); medium-term (application phase — SMEs applying improvements, route operational, branding visible, post-project governance in design); long-term (systemic — auto-sustained route, SME competitiveness consolidated, twin transition embedded, intangible heritage valorised, model replicated in other European territories). KPIs must be quantified, with units, baseline and target, and aligned with the EU Tourism Dashboard.
questions.connects_from: the needs and objectives in 1.2; the methodology in 2.1.1; the WP structure in 4.1; the FSTP scheme.
questions.connects_to: the monitoring system in 2.1.2; the dissemination in 3.2; the sustainability in 3.3; the replicability in WP4.
questions.global_rule: Always quantify impact with EU Tourism Dashboard-aligned KPIs. Always articulate short, medium and long-term layers. Always identify the route ecosystem as the main impact vehicle.
questions.writing_guidance: This section belongs to the Impact criterion of the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME call. It must define the measurable transformation the project generates in the route ecosystem and the wider European tourism sector, identifying specific target groups and articulating short, medium and long-term impact pathways.

questions.title: Consortium set-up
questions.description:
Please address all guiding points presented in the Call document/Programme Guide under the award criterion Quality of the partnership and the cooperation arrangements.

Describe the participants (Beneficiaries, Affiliated Entities, Associated Partners and others, if any) and explain how they will work together to implement the project. How will they bring together the necessary expertise? How will they complement each other?

In what way does each of the participants contribute to the project? Show that each has a valid role and adequate resources to fulfil that role.
questions.general_context: The answer must introduce the consortium as a functionally complementary structure that mirrors the route ecosystem. It must clearly identify: the 3 DMOs (Destination Management Organisations) and explain their role in route co-design, territorial coordination and stakeholder engagement; the 3 BSOs (Business Support Organisations) and explain their role in SME outreach, FSTP design and delivery, capacity building, and mentoring. CRITICAL: tourism SMEs are NOT consortium partners — they are recipients of FSTP support. The answer must explain how partners complement each other: DMOs bring tourism and territorial expertise, BSOs bring SME support and FSTP delivery capacity, the coordinator brings overall management and replicability leadership. It must describe the cooperation logic: how partners work through structured coordination mechanisms (Consortium Agreement, Steering Committee, WP leaders, joint meetings, digital platforms). It must define each partner's role linked to specific WPs and demonstrate that each has the resources, networks and experience required.
questions.connects_from: the objectives in 1.2; the methodology in 2.1.1; the WP structure in 4.1; the consortium composition mandatory criteria of the call.
questions.connects_to: the management system in 2.1.2; the team description in 2.1.3; the financial management in 2.1.4; the consortium management in 2.2.2; the sustainability plan in 3.3.
questions.global_rule: Tourism SMEs are NEVER consortium partners — they are FSTP recipients. Always specify which entities are DMOs and which are BSOs according to the call's definitions. Always confirm compliance with the 3 DMOs / 2 BSOs / 2 countries each minimum.
questions.writing_guidance: This section belongs to the Quality criterion of the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME call (sub-criterion: Quality of the partnership and cooperation arrangements). It must demonstrate that the consortium is strategically composed to deliver a transnational tourism route ecosystem with FSTP, complying with the mandatory eligibility criteria of the call: minimum 3 DMOs from at least 2 different countries + minimum 2 BSOs from at least 2 different countries (total 5-8 entities).

questions.title: Work plan
questions.description: Provide a brief description of the overall structure of the work plan (list of work packages or graphical presentation (Pert chart or similar)).
questions.general_context: The SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME call imposes a MANDATORY structure of 4 Work Packages with predefined thematic content. The work plan MUST follow this structure. Coordination is a transversal management function, NOT a separate WP. The 4 mandatory WPs are: WP1 — Establishment or Redesign of the Route and the Ecosystem; WP2 — Development and Engagement of the Ecosystem (with mandatory sub-component WP2.2 Financial Support to Third Parties); WP3 — Communication, Promotion and Branding of the Route Ecosystem; WP4 — Production of Best Practices and Case Studies for Replicability. Coordination tasks and costs are typically distributed within the 4 mandatory WPs or covered through dedicated coordination effort within the budget — NEVER as a 5th WP. If the Application Form structurally requires a dedicated coordination/management section, it should be framed as Project Management within the proposal (separate from the 4 mandatory WPs) rather than as WP5.
questions.global_rule: NEVER create a 5th WP for coordination — the call imposes 4 mandatory thematic WPs. Coordination is transversal. Always respect the mandatory thematic content and timeline of each WP.
questions.writing_guidance: Provide a brief description of the overall structure of the work plan (list of work packages or graphical presentation — Pert chart or similar), strictly following the call's mandatory 4-WP architecture (WP1 Route, WP2 Ecosystem development with FSTP, WP3 Communication & branding, WP4 Best practices & replicability).

questions.title: Concept and methodology
questions.description:
Please address all guiding points presented in the Call document/Programme Guide under the award criterion Quality of the project design and implementation.

Outline the approach and methodology behind the project. Explain why they are the most suitable for achieving the project's objectives.
questions.general_context: The answer must present the central concept of the project: a transnational tourism route that mobilises and strengthens an ecosystem of SMEs through a combination of route co-design (WP1), capacity building and FSTP (WP2), strategic communication and branding (WP3), and replicability (WP4). The methodology must be a deliberate, structured choice: co-creation with stakeholders, open and transparent FSTP for SMEs, mentoring and capacity building, applied piloting in real tourism contexts, peer learning between cross-border partners, and continuous engagement with regional/local authorities and intangible heritage carriers. The operational logic must be phased: M1-M6 route co-design and ecosystem mapping; M6-M12 FSTP open call and SME selection; M12-M24 FSTP implementation and capacity building; M24-M36 evaluation, replicability and consolidation of post-project governance. Each partner must have a defined role based on its strengths: DMOs lead route design and territorial coordination, BSOs (including Chambers) lead FSTP design and SME support, the coordinator ensures overall consistency. The justification must explain why this approach is more effective than alternatives: integration of route and SME support in one model, twin transition acceleration through targeted FSTP, transferability through cross-border testing.
questions.connects_from: the problem and needs in 1.1 and 1.2; the specific objectives and indicators; the consortium composition in 2.2.1; the call's mandatory WP structure.
questions.connects_to: the work plan in 4.1 (4 mandatory WPs); the management and monitoring system in 2.1.2; the team description in 2.1.3; the risk analysis in 2.1.5; the impact narrative in 3.1; the sustainability plan in 3.3.
questions.global_rule: The concept must always be the route ecosystem, not generic SME support. The methodology must always integrate FSTP as a structural component.
questions.writing_guidance: This section belongs to the Quality criterion of the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME call. It explains how the project is structured to operate in practice and how it will achieve its objectives in a coherent, realistic and methodologically sound way, with the tourism route ecosystem and its SMEs at the centre. While 1.x explains why the project is needed, this section explains how it operates.

questions.title: Ethics
questions.description:
Describe any ethics issues that may arise during the project implementation and the measures to address them.

Address gender mainstreaming and children's rights where applicable.

questions.title: Project Summary
questions.description: Provide a brief overview of the project.

questions.title: Background and general objectives
questions.description:
Please address all guiding points presented in the Call document/Programme Guide under the award criterion Relevance.

Describe the background and rationale of the project.

How is the project relevant to the scope of the call? How does the project address the general objectives of the call? What is the project's contribution to the priorities of the call (if applicable)?
questions.general_context: The answer must be written in clear paragraphs, never in bullet points, and follow a strict logical sequence adapted to the tourism SME ecosystem and route-based interventions. The first paragraph must describe the structural problem affecting tourism SMEs in the European tourism ecosystem, including low adoption of sustainable practices, insufficient digitalisation, weak resilience to crises (COVID-19 legacy, inflation, climate impacts), excessive concentration of tourism flows in specific destinations (over-tourism) alongside under-utilisation of rural and remote territories (under-tourism), seasonality, and skills gaps related to the twin transition. The paragraph must clearly state who is affected (tourism SMEs in specific territorial typologies — rural, coastal, mountain, cross-border, less-developed regions) and in what context (post-COVID recovery, twin transition pressures, increasing demand for sustainable and authentic experiences). The second paragraph must support this with data and evidence drawn from sources such as the European Commission Transition Pathway for Tourism, Eurostat tourism statistics, the EU Tourism Dashboard, OECD Tourism reports, and UNWTO publications. It must demonstrate that the problem is policy-recognised at EU level, not an opinion. The third paragraph must explain urgency: the green and digital transitions of tourism are accelerating, EU policy frameworks (European Green Deal, Transition Pathway for Tourism, European Agenda for Tourism 2030, Digital Compass 2030) are placing increasing demands on SMEs, and the competitive position of European tourism depends on how quickly the SME fabric adapts. The fourth paragraph is the rationale for intervention: tourism SMEs cannot solve these challenges alone due to limited resources, expertise, and fragmentation; isolated local actions are insufficient; transnational cooperation enables shared methodologies, peer learning, transferability and scale. Finally, the answer must explicitly link to the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME objectives: creation of transnational thematic and/or geographical tourism routes, mobilisation and strengthening of the route ecosystem, balanced redistribution of tourism flows addressing both over-tourism and under-tourism, diversification of supply and reduction of seasonality, regenerative tourism that respects authenticity, valorisation of intangible cultural heritage, and well-being of local communities. The European added value must be made explicit: shared tools and methodologies, cross-border learning, transferability across destinations, and aggregation of impact at European scale.
questions.connects_from: real challenges faced by tourism SMEs in the targeted territories; evidence from EU tourism and SME reports; understanding of the twin transition in tourism; alignment with EU tourism policies and COSME priorities.
questions.connects_to: the answer of 1.2 (needs analysis and specific objectives — each objective must respond to a problem identified here); the answer of 1.3 (complementarity and European added value); the design of WP1 (route establishment and ecosystem mapping); the impact narrative in 3.1; the consortium justification in 2.2.1.
questions.global_rule: Always frame the problem at European level (not only at the project's territorial scope), and always conclude with how the project specifically addresses the call objectives.
questions.writing_guidance: This section belongs to the Relevance criterion of the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME call, managed by EISMEA under the Single Market Programme. It focuses on explaining the overall logic of the project at its earliest stage, specifically within the European tourism ecosystem and the network of tourism SMEs that will be supported through a transnational tourism route. Its purpose is to clearly demonstrate why tourism SMEs in specific European territories need targeted support now, what is structurally failing in terms of sustainability, resilience and competitiveness, and how the project responds directly to this European challenge by establishing a thematic and/or geographical route that mobilises and strengthens the ecosystem. It must show that the project is not an isolated initiative but a coherent response to structural weaknesses recognised at EU level. This section sets the strategic foundation of the proposal.

questions.title: Needs analysis and specific objectives
questions.description:
Please address the specific conditions/objectives set out in the Call document/Programme Guide, if applicable.

Describe how the objectives of the project are based on a sound needs analysis in line with the specific objectives of the call. What issue/challenge/gap does the project aim to address?

The objectives should be clear, measurable, realistic and achievable within the duration of the project. For each objective, define appropriate indicators for measuring achievement (including a unit of measurement, baseline value and target value).
questions.general_context: The answer must explain how the project is based on a structured needs analysis that identifies real, evidence-based gaps in the tourism SME ecosystem along the proposed route. It must go deeper than the previous section, identifying what is concretely missing in terms of capacity, knowledge, tools, infrastructure or support systems, and why these gaps persist. The needs analysis must operate at two levels. First, the needs of the target groups, primarily tourism SMEs that will be reached through FSTP and capacity building: access to applicable sustainability and digital tools, capacity to adapt business models for regenerative practices, ability to participate in structured route offers, accessibility upgrades, energy efficiency, and integration into cross-border value chains. Second, the needs of participating organisations: DMOs need updated methodological frameworks for managing transnational routes, BSOs need to strengthen their capacity to design and operate FSTP schemes and to deliver tailored SME support. These organisational needs must be linked to each partner's role in the project. The section must then explain how identified needs are translated into specific objectives. Objectives must describe concrete changes (not activities) — increased SME capacity, strengthened support mechanisms, enhanced ecosystem cooperation. Each objective must include indicators with unit of measurement, baseline value and target value, and must be aligned with the EU Tourism Dashboard framework wherever possible. Finally, the section must demonstrate that the identified needs represent a broader European challenge: similar gaps exist across European destinations, justifying a transnational approach and ensuring transferability.
questions.connects_from: the problem definition built in 1.1; evidence from EU tourism reports; characteristics of the route territories; the specific objectives of the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME call; the strengths and gaps of each partner organisation.
questions.connects_to: the formulation of indicators (must be measurable and aligned with EU Tourism Dashboard); the design of WP1 (route definition based on needs); the design of WP2 (FSTP and capacity building responding to SME needs); the impact narrative in 3.1; the monitoring and evaluation system in 2.1.2; the work plan in 4.1.
questions.global_rule: Every objective must be SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound), must include indicators with baseline and target values, and must be a direct response to a need identified in the analysis.
questions.writing_guidance: This section belongs to the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME call under the Relevance criterion, specifically focusing on the needs analysis and specific objectives within the tourism SME ecosystem of the targeted route territories. It must demonstrate that the project is built on a clear, structured, evidence-based understanding of real needs affecting tourism SMEs, the partner organisations (DMOs and BSOs) that will support them, and the wider territorial ecosystem. The project must be problem-driven, not activity-driven, and its objectives must be a direct response to identified gaps related to sustainability, resilience, competitiveness, twin transition, and territorial balance. The section also establishes the basis for SMART objectives with indicators aligned with the EU Tourism Dashboard.

questions.title: Work packages, activities, resources and timing
questions.description:
Group your activities into work packages. A work package means a major sub-division of the project.

For each work package, enter an objective (expected outcome) and list the activities, milestones and deliverables that belong to it.

Projects should normally have a minimum of 2 work packages. WP1 should cover the management and coordination activities. WP2 and further WPs should be used for the other project activities. The last WP should be dedicated to Impact and dissemination.

Work packages covering financial support to third parties only allowed if authorised in the Call document/Programme Guide.

Enter each activity/milestone/output/outcome/deliverable only once (under one work package).

The Coordinator remains fully responsible for the coordination tasks, even if they are delegated to someone else. Coordinator tasks cannot be subcontracted.
questions.general_context: Each of the 4 mandatory WPs must be described with: (i) a clear intention/purpose statement, (ii) the substantive elements (tasks T_x.y), (iii) deliverables (D_x.y), (iv) milestones (M_x.y) with month references, and (v) explicit alignment with the call's mandatory thematic content and FSTP/visibility/replicability obligations. WP1 (M1-M12 or M1-M9) defines the route as a real, operational tourism product and maps the full ecosystem. WP2 (M6-M30) is the operational core: WP2.1 Engagement and capacity building (no direct funding to participants) + WP2.2 Mandatory FSTP cascade funding to 10-30 SMEs at 7,000-15,000 € lump sums each, fully compliant with EU FSTP good practice (open transparent call ≥2 months, independent evaluation committee with conflict of interest declarations, double-funding declarations, milestone-based payments, audit trail 5 years). WP3 (M3-M36) provides the route's public face including the 2 mandatory short videos (1-2 min, one at M3, one at M30) and the mandatory Brussels event (2 days, 5 people), with EU branding compliant under SMP-AGA. WP4 (M18-M36) capitalises experience into a replicability toolkit (open-access license), case studies, peer-learning webinars, ≥2 policy briefs and the mandatory publication on the EU Tourism Platform, with archive ≥5 years per SMP-AGA Article 22.
questions.global_rule: Each WP must respect: the call's mandatory thematic content; the FSTP good-practice rules in WP2.2 (transparent call, conflict of interest, double-funding, audit trail); the mandatory deliverables in WP3 (2 videos + Brussels event + EU emblem); the mandatory EU Tourism Platform publication and 5-year archive in WP4.
questions.writing_guidance: Describe each Work Package separately, fully respecting the call's mandatory 4-WP architecture. Each WP description must include intention, tasks, deliverables, milestones, and explicit alignment with mandatory call obligations (FSTP rules in WP2.2, mandatory videos and Brussels event in WP3, EU Tourism Platform publication in WP4).

questions.title: Consortium management and decision-making
questions.description:
Explain the management structures and decision-making mechanisms within the consortium. Describe how decisions will be taken and how regular and effective communication will be ensured. Describe methods to ensure planning and control.

Note: The concept (including organisational structure and decision-making mechanisms) must be adapted to the complexity and scale of the project.
questions.general_context: The answer must describe: (a) the overall governance structure (Coordinator, Steering Committee with all 6 partners, WP leaders, FSTP independent evaluation committee); (b) decision-making mechanisms (operational decisions by WP leaders; strategic decisions by Steering Committee through consensus or qualified majority fallback; conflict resolution and escalation procedures); (c) the Consortium Agreement signed by M1 codifying these mechanisms; (d) communication mechanisms (monthly online coordination calls per WP, quarterly Steering Committee meetings, biannual in-person meetings rotating between Spain and France, shared digital platform, structured reporting templates, English as working language with multilingual SME-facing materials); (e) planning and control (Gantt with milestones, monthly progress reports, integration with SMP reporting cycle). The structure must be proportionate — neither over-engineered nor flat.
questions.connects_from: the consortium composition in 2.2.1; the WP structure in 4.1; the management system in 2.1.2; the methodology in 2.1.1.
questions.connects_to: the risk management in 2.1.5; the financial management in 2.1.4; the EISMEA reporting cycle; the sustainability plan in 3.3 (post-project governance).
questions.global_rule: Always describe the Consortium Agreement signed by M1. Always confirm the governance is proportionate to the project's scale. Always reference SMP/EISMEA, never EACEA/Erasmus+.
questions.writing_guidance: This section belongs to the Quality criterion of the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME call (Quality of the partnership and cooperation arrangements). It must demonstrate that the consortium operates through a clear, structured and efficient governance system proportionate to a 36-month, 1.4 M€ project involving 6 partners across 2 countries with FSTP delivery to 30 tourism SMEs.

questions.title: Security
questions.description: Describe any security issues if applicable.

questions.title: Project management, quality assurance and monitoring and evaluation strategy
questions.description:
Describe the measures foreseen to ensure that the project implementation is of high quality and completed in time.

Describe the methods to ensure good quality, monitoring, planning and control.

Describe the evaluation methods and indicators (quantitative and qualitative) to monitor and verify the outreach and coverage of the activities and results (including unit of measurement, baseline and target values). The indicators proposed to measure progress should be relevant, realistic and measurable.
questions.general_context: The answer must describe the project management approach with the role of the coordinator, the governance bodies (Steering Committee with all partners, WP leaders), decision-making protocols, and communication mechanisms (regular online and in-person meetings, shared digital platform, structured reporting templates). It must describe the quality assurance system: internal review of deliverables, peer validation across partners, feedback from FSTP recipients, continuous improvement cycles. It must describe monitoring of progress through milestones, deliverables tracking, KPIs aligned with the EU Tourism Dashboard, and integration with the SMP reporting cycle (interim and final reports to EISMEA). It must describe the evaluation strategy with quantitative indicators (number of SMEs supported, % adoption of digital tools, jobs created/maintained, tourism flows to under-visited territories, etc.) and qualitative indicators (SME satisfaction, stakeholder engagement, governance maturity), including data collection methods (surveys, attendance records, FSTP recipient reports, pilot outcomes).
questions.connects_from: the objectives and indicators in 1.2; the methodology in 2.1.1; the partnership structure in 2.2.1; the work plan in 4.1; the SMP Annotated Grant Agreement framework.
questions.connects_to: the risk management in 2.1.5; the consortium management in 2.2.2; the reporting cycle to EISMEA; the impact narrative in 3.1.
questions.global_rule: Always link KPIs to the EU Tourism Dashboard categories when possible. Always treat coordination as transversal, not as a separate WP. Always reference SMP/EISMEA, never Erasmus+/EACEA.
questions.writing_guidance: This section belongs to the Quality criterion of the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME call. It must demonstrate that the project is governed by a structured management, quality assurance, monitoring and evaluation system aligned with the requirements of the SMP Annotated Grant Agreement and EISMEA reporting obligations. Coordination is treated as a transversal management function (not a separate WP).

questions.title: Communication, dissemination and visibility
questions.description:
Describe the communication and dissemination activities which are planned in order to promote the activities/results and maximise the impact (to whom, which format, how many, etc.). Clarify how you will reach the target groups, relevant stakeholders, policymakers and the general public and explain the choice of the dissemination channels.

Describe how the visibility of EU funding will be ensured.
questions.general_context: The answer must define: (a) target audiences — tourism SMEs (FSTP recipients and broader pool), DMOs and BSOs across Europe, policymakers (national tourism authorities, EISMEA, DG GROW), territorial stakeholders, tour operators, travellers, general public; (b) content — route brand and offer, FSTP results, SME case studies, best practices toolkit, policy briefs; (c) channels and formats — project website (multilingual ES/FR/EN/EU), social media (Instagram, LinkedIn, TikTok where relevant for younger audiences), digital newsletters, partner channels, Chamber networks, EU Tourism Platform publications, EU Tourism Platform events, sectoral conferences, the mandatory Brussels event (2 days, 5 people), the mandatory 2 short videos (1-2 min each), policy briefs. The answer must articulate communication across the project lifecycle (pre-implementation awareness, in-implementation continuous updates, post-implementation scaling). Visibility of EU funding must follow SMP-AGA rules: "Funded by the European Union" emblem with disclaimer on all materials; FSTP support presented as "co-funded by the EU" (not direct EU funding to SMEs); use of EC Communication Indicators for measurement.
questions.connects_from: the objectives in 1.2; the WP3 design in 4.1; the partnership in 2.2.1; the SMP-AGA visibility rules.
questions.connects_to: the impact narrative in 3.1; the sustainability in 3.3; the EU Tourism Platform publications in WP4; the mandatory Brussels event participation.
questions.global_rule: Always use the "Funded by the European Union" emblem, NEVER the Erasmus+ logo (this is SMP-COSME). Always reference the Destination Europe framework, Visit Europe portal, EU Tourism Platform, EC Communication Indicators. Always include the mandatory 2 videos and Brussels event.
questions.writing_guidance: This section belongs to the Impact criterion of the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME call. It must describe how project results are strategically communicated, disseminated and given visibility, fully aligned with the Destination Europe framework, the EU Tourism Platform, the Visit Europe portal and national tourism portals (visit France, Spain.info), and complying with SMP-AGA visibility obligations including the "Funded by the European Union" emblem and disclaimer.

questions.title: Sustainability and continuation
questions.description:
Describe the follow-up of the project after the EU funding ends. How will the project impact be ensured and sustained?

What will need to be done? Which parts of the project should be continued or maintained? How will this be achieved? Which resources will be necessary to continue the project? How will the results be used?

Are there any possible synergies/complementarities with other (EU funded) activities that can build on the project results?
questions.general_context: The answer must articulate sustainability at three levels. (1) Economic sustainability of the ecosystem: each FSTP-supported SME depends economically on visitor flows the route generates, providing a natural incentive to maintain the route alive without continued subsidies; SME fees, booking commissions through the platform, and tour operator partnerships generate revenue for the post-project entity. (2) Legal/governance sustainability: a post-project entity (the answer must evaluate three options — transnational not-for-profit association, EGTC particularly attractive for cross-border projects, or AEIE) constituted during the last 12 months of the project and operational at M36 ensures continuity of brand, platform, quality standards, and ecosystem cohesion. (3) Strategic sustainability: integration into national tourism strategies, follow-up funding pathways (Interreg POCTEFA, LIFE, next SMP-COSME windows, LEADER, ERDF), replicability toolkit published on the EU Tourism Platform, peer-learning with other European route initiatives.
questions.connects_from: the objectives in 1.2; the methodology in 2.1.1; the partnership in 2.2.1; the impact in 3.1.
questions.connects_to: the impact narrative in 3.1 (long-term layer); the WP4 design (best practices and replicability); the EU added value in 1.3.
questions.global_rule: Always describe the post-project legal entity (with options evaluated). Always explain financial sustainability with concrete revenue streams. Always identify follow-up EU funding pathways. NEVER claim sustainability without operational mechanisms.
questions.writing_guidance: This section belongs to the Impact criterion of the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME call. It must demonstrate that the project creates lasting value: that the route, the SME network, the digital platform, and the partner cooperation continue beyond EU funding, supported by a post-project governance structure (e.g. EGTC, AEIE or transnational association) and a credible financial sustainability plan.

questions.title: Complementarity with other actions and innovation - European added value
questions.description:
Explain how the project builds on the results of past activities carried out in the field, and describe its innovative aspects (if any).

Explain how the activities are complementary to other activities carried out by other organisations (if applicable). Illustrate the trans-national dimension of the project; its impact/interest in the EU area; possibility to use the results in other countries, potential to develop cross-border cooperation among Programme countries and Partner countries, if applicable.

If your proposal is based on the results of one or more previous or ongoing projects, please provide precise references to these projects.
questions.general_context: The answer must explain how the project is rooted in existing knowledge, prior projects and accumulated experience in the tourism, SME support, and territorial cooperation fields. Specific references to previous or ongoing projects (Interreg, LIFE, COSME, Horizon Europe, LEADER, Erasmus+ — as continuation pathways, not as the funding programme of this proposal) must be provided when applicable, with explanation of what concrete results, tools or methodologies are being reused, adapted or improved. The answer must clearly describe the innovative aspects of the project: in this call, innovation typically takes the form of integrated route-ecosystem models combining FSTP, governance, branding and replicability; new methodologies for cross-border SME engagement; integration of digital tools (booking platforms, AR experiences, smart tourism data) into rural and regenerative tourism contexts; novel models of governance ensuring auto-sustainability of the route post-project (associations, EGTCs, AEIEs). The answer must explain complementarity with other EU initiatives (Destination Europe framework, EU Tourism Platform, Enterprise Europe Network, European Digital Innovation Hubs, European Cluster Collaboration Platform, Pact for Skills, PANTOUR) showing that the project does not duplicate but rather connects and amplifies. Finally, the answer must articulate the transnational dimension and European added value through cross-border ecosystem cooperation, transferability of route models, peer learning between DMOs and BSOs, and scaling potential to other European destinations.
questions.connects_from: the problem and needs identified in 1.1 and 1.2; prior project experience of consortium partners; existing EU tourism networks and platforms; the call's specific framework.
questions.connects_to: the methodology design in 2.1.1; the partnership justification in 2.2.1; the dissemination strategy in 3.2; the sustainability plan in 3.3; the WP4 design (best practices and replicability) in 4.1.
questions.global_rule: Always cite concrete previous projects when they exist; abstract claims of "building on previous experience" without references are unconvincing. Always explain what is new versus what is built upon.
questions.writing_guidance: This section belongs to the Relevance criterion of the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME call. It must show that the project capitalises on existing knowledge, tools and networks within the European tourism ecosystem, introduces clear methodological or operational innovation in the design and operation of a transnational tourism route, is complementary (not duplicative) to other ongoing EU initiatives in tourism, and generates demonstrable European added value through cross-border cooperation.

questions.title: Project teams, staff and experts
questions.description:
Describe the project teams and how they will work together to implement the project.

List the staff included in the project budget (budget category A) by function/profile and describe briefly their tasks. Provide CVs of all key actors (if required by the Call document/Programme Guide).
questions.general_context: The answer must describe the project team as an integrated system. It must cover: management capacity (coordinator, project manager, financial officer); thematic expertise in tourism and route management (DMO staff); SME support and FSTP design and delivery expertise (BSO staff, with proven experience in cascade funding); sustainability and twin transition expertise (sustainability experts, digital experts); communication and branding expertise (communication officers); monitoring and evaluation expertise. It must explain how partners coordinate internally and across the consortium, the staff profiles aligned with SMP budget category A (Personnel costs A.1 employees, A.2 natural persons, A.3 seconded, A.4 SME owners), and the demonstrated experience in EU-funded projects.
questions.connects_from: the methodology in 2.1.1; the WP structure in 4.1; the partnership in 2.2.1; the budget categories defined in the call.
questions.connects_to: the management system in 2.1.2; the consortium management in 2.2.2; the budget breakdown; the feasibility narrative in 2.1.5.
questions.global_rule: Always link team profiles to SMP budget category A. Always demonstrate FSTP-specific experience for BSO staff. Always include CV availability for key actors.
questions.writing_guidance: This section belongs to the Quality criterion of the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME call. It must demonstrate that the consortium has the human resources required to deliver a transnational route ecosystem project including FSTP for 30 SMEs, with complementary expertise across tourism, SME support, sustainability, digital, communication, monitoring and financial management.

questions.title: Cost effectiveness and financial management
questions.description:
Describe the measures adopted to ensure that the proposed results and objectives will be achieved in the most cost-effective way.

Indicate the arrangements adopted for the financial management of the project and, in particular, how the financial resources will be allocated and managed within the consortium.

Do NOT compare and justify the costs of each work package, but summarize briefly why your budget is cost effective.
questions.general_context: The answer must describe the financial management system: the coordinator's role in centralised financial coordination and reporting to EISMEA; each partner's responsibility for managing its allocated budget under SMP-AGA rules and reporting standards; the use of standardised templates for cost reporting; reporting cycles aligned with the SMP reporting structure (interim and final reports); and the specific arrangements for the FSTP budget line (D.1) including SME selection, lump-sum payments (7,000-15,000 €), and double-funding declarations. The cost-effectiveness narrative must explain how the consortium maximises results within the 1,200,000-1,400,000 € budget envelope: leveraging existing partner infrastructure, shared digital tools, online and hybrid working modalities, and concentrating funding where it delivers most value (the FSTP, ~27% of budget, going directly to SMEs at 100% co-financing).
questions.connects_from: the objectives in 1.2; the work plan in 4.1; the partnership in 2.2.1; the SMP-AGA framework; the FSTP rules of the call.
questions.connects_to: the detailed budget table (Excel annex); the financial sustainability in 3.3; the risk register in 2.1.5; the EISMEA reporting cycle.
questions.global_rule: Always reference SMP-AGA and EISMEA, never EACEA. Always mention the dual co-financing rate (100% for FSTP / 90% for other categories). Always frame cost-effectiveness in terms of concentrating value on the FSTP and using digital/hybrid modalities.
questions.writing_guidance: This section belongs to the Quality criterion of the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME call. It must demonstrate that the project applies cost-effective principles and that the consortium can manage SMP financial resources transparently and in compliance with the Annotated Grant Agreement (AGA), including the specific rules for FSTP (cost category D.1, 100% co-financing rate) and other categories (90% co-financing rate).

questions.title: Risk management
questions.description:
Describe critical risks, uncertainties or difficulties related to the implementation of your project, and your measures/strategy for addressing them.

Indicate for each risk (in the description) the impact and the likelihood that the risk will materialise (high, medium, low), even after taking into account the mitigating measures.

Note: Uncertainties and unexpected events occur in all organisations, even if very well-run. The risk analysis will help you to predict issues that could delay or hinder project activities.
questions.general_context: Risks for this specific call include: (a) operational risks — PIC validation delays, low SME participation in FSTP open call, partner withdrawal, delays in route co-design; (b) FSTP-specific risks — conflict of interest in SME selection, double-funding declarations failures, lump-sum milestone non-achievement, low SME absorption capacity; (c) sector-specific risks — tourism seasonality affecting SME availability for capacity building, market volatility, climate events affecting tourism flows; (d) governance risks — failure to consolidate post-project governance, partner coordination challenges across borders; (e) external risks — policy changes, economic downturns, sanitary crises. Each risk must have a likelihood and impact rating (low/medium/high) AFTER mitigation, must be linked to a specific WP, and must have realistic mitigation measures adapted to the route ecosystem and tourism SME reality.
questions.connects_from: the WP structure in 4.1; the methodology in 2.1.1; the partnership in 2.2.1; the FSTP scheme rules; the SMP-AGA framework.
questions.connects_to: the management system in 2.1.2; the sustainability plan in 3.3; the financial management in 2.1.4; the EISMEA reporting cycle.
questions.global_rule: Always include FSTP-specific risks (conflict of interest, double funding, low absorption capacity). Always link each risk to a specific WP. Always rate likelihood and impact AFTER mitigation.
questions.writing_guidance: This section belongs to the Quality criterion of the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME call. It must demonstrate that the consortium has systematically identified critical risks specific to a transnational tourism route ecosystem project with FSTP delivery, and that mitigation measures are proportionate and operational. Length limit: Risk register with 8-12 risks (table format) plus brief introductory text.

criteria.title: Building on previous experience and results
criteria.intent: To demonstrate that the project is grounded in existing knowledge and proven approaches in the tourism and SME support fields, ensuring continuity, learning, and avoidance of duplication.
criteria.elements: The answer must reference specific prior or ongoing projects (with name, programme, dates) and explain what concrete results, tools or methodologies are being reused, adapted or improved. It must go beyond listing — it must show direct relevance to the current proposal. It must highlight how partner experience contributes to project feasibility and quality.
criteria.example_weak: Listing several previous projects without explaining how they connect to the current proposal.
criteria.example_strong: The project builds on the Interreg POCTEFA project [acronym] (2022-2024), which developed a validated methodology for cross-border SME engagement in tourism, and on the COSME-funded [acronym] project, which produced a digital toolkit for sustainability adoption. Both methodologies are adapted and integrated into the SUSTRAI route ecosystem framework.
criteria.avoid: Avoid name-dropping projects without explaining their relevance. Avoid including projects that have no direct connection to the proposal.

criteria.title: Sectoral problem definition in the tourism SME ecosystem
criteria.intent: This criterion ensures that the answer identifies a real and concrete problem affecting tourism SMEs in connection with the establishment or reinforcement of a tourism route. Evaluators must immediately understand what is structurally failing and why SMEs in specific territories are unable to compete sustainably.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe a specific and territorially grounded problem (for example: rural tourism SMEs lack the technical and financial capacity to implement sustainable practices and to integrate into structured tourism offers; coastal destinations face over-tourism while their rural backlands face under-tourism and depopulation; intangible heritage trades are at risk of disappearing). It must clearly define who is affected (tourism SMEs by typology and territory), where (specific European destinations: rural, coastal, mountain, cross-border), and in what context (twin transition pressures, post-pandemic recovery, climate adaptation).
criteria.example_weak: Tourism is important and needs to be more sustainable.
criteria.example_strong: In rural and seasonal European destinations, the tourism SME fabric (small accommodations, artisanal food producers, experience operators) lacks the financial and technical capacity to implement sustainable practices and integrate into competitive transnational tourism offers, limiting territorial cohesion and accelerating depopulation of inland areas while coastal cores absorb disproportionate tourism flows.
criteria.avoid: Avoid general statements about tourism. Do not introduce solutions or project activities at this stage. Do not describe the project itself — describe the problem the project responds to.

criteria.title: Evidence-based sectoral gap analysis in the route ecosystem
criteria.intent: To ensure that the project is based on a clearly identified, evidence-based gap in the tourism SME ecosystem along the proposed route, justifying intervention at European level.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe specific problems affecting tourism SMEs in the targeted territories (limited capacity to implement sustainable practices, insufficient access to tailored support mechanisms, lack of digital tools, isolation from cross-border value chains, intangible heritage trades at risk). The gap must be contextualised (who is affected, under what conditions), supported with relevant European evidence (statistics, EU Tourism Dashboard data, Commission reports), and explained in terms of why the gap persists (structural barriers, limited resources, fragmentation, lack of coordination).
criteria.example_weak: Stating that tourism SMEs face sustainability challenges without specifying which challenges, who is affected, or any supporting data.
criteria.example_strong: Explaining that a large proportion of tourism SMEs in rural and cross-border territories lack the technical and financial capacity to implement sustainable practices, as documented by EU Tourism Dashboard indicators on SME digitalisation and green adoption, which limits their integration into competitive transnational route offers and accelerates territorial depopulation.
criteria.avoid: Avoid general statements about tourism or sustainability without a specific gap. Avoid introducing activities or solutions at this stage. Avoid evidence that is not from credible EU-level sources.

criteria.title: WP1 — Establishment or Redesign of the Route and the Ecosystem
criteria.intent: WP1 is the foundational WP of the project. It defines the route as a real, operational tourism product and maps the full ecosystem of stakeholders that will sustain it. Evaluators expect to see a clear methodological route co-design process with stakeholder engagement, geographic and thematic definition of the route, governance design, KPI baseline aligned with the EU Tourism Dashboard, and a business plan for the route's post-project sustainability.
criteria.elements: WP1 must be structured around route co-design and ecosystem mapping. It typically runs M1-M12 (or M1-M9 for an accelerated design). Tasks must be designed around the following core route-establishment functions: T1.1 route concept refinement and narrative finalisation; T1.2 detailed physical route design (corridor, micro-routes, points of interest, signage); T1.3 ecosystem mapping (SMEs by typology, stakeholders, authorities, intangible heritage carriers); T1.4 stakeholder engagement and Letters of Intent collection from local authorities, parks, associations; T1.5 governance design for the route (consortium-level governance + design of post-project entity); T1.6 KPI baseline aligned with the EU Tourism Dashboard; T1.7 business plan for the route (post-project financial sustainability); T1.8 alignment with Destination Europe framework, Visit Europe portal, and national portals. Deliverables must include: D1.1 Route concept and narrative document; D1.2 Detailed route map and design; D1.3 Ecosystem mapping report; D1.4 Stakeholder engagement report and Letters of Intent compilation; D1.5 Governance design document (including post-project entity option analysis); D1.6 KPI baseline report aligned with EU Tourism Dashboard; D1.7 Route business plan; D1.8 Destination Europe framework alignment note. Milestones: M1.1 Route concept validated (M3); M1.2 Detailed route design approved (M6); M1.3 Ecosystem mapping completed (M6); M1.4 Letters of Intent secured (target: 12+) (M9); M1.5 Governance structure designed (M9); M1.6 KPI baseline established (M9); M1.7 Route business plan validated (M12).
criteria.example_weak: WP1 listed as "project preparation and management" without route-specific content, ecosystem mapping, or alignment with the call's mandatory route concept.
criteria.example_strong: WP1 fully focused on transnational tourism route co-design, with explicit tasks covering route narrative, physical design, ecosystem mapping in both countries, stakeholder Letters of Intent, governance design including the post-project entity, KPI baseline aligned with the EU Tourism Dashboard, and a route business plan.
criteria.avoid: Do NOT make WP1 a generic project management WP. Do NOT omit the route as the central concept. Do NOT skip stakeholder engagement and Letters of Intent collection. Do NOT omit alignment with Destination Europe framework.

criteria.title: Risk description — sector-specific and FSTP-specific risks
criteria.intent: To ensure each risk is described concretely in terms of the route ecosystem, tourism SME reality, and FSTP delivery, not in generic project management terms.
criteria.elements: Each risk in the register must include: what the risk is, why it may occur in this specific context (route ecosystem, FSTP, transnational cooperation), how it could affect implementation or results. Descriptions must be grounded in tourism SME realities (seasonality, micro-enterprise constraints) and FSTP-specific challenges (transparency, conflict of interest, low absorption).
criteria.example_weak: Risk of delay in project implementation.
criteria.example_strong: Risk: low participation of tourism SMEs in the FSTP open call due to perceived administrative burden, micro-enterprise time constraints, and seasonal workload peaks (June-September), which could result in fewer than 25 SMEs selected and reduced FSTP impact.
criteria.avoid: Avoid generic risk descriptions. Avoid ignoring FSTP-specific risks. Avoid risks unrelated to the tourism ecosystem.

criteria.title: Route ecosystem concept and intervention logic
criteria.intent: To ensure that the project is built around a clear and structured concept centred on the transnational tourism route and its SME ecosystem.
criteria.elements: The answer must clearly define the central concept (a transnational thematic and/or geographical tourism route mobilising an ecosystem of tourism SMEs, partner DMOs and BSOs, and territorial stakeholders), explain the intervention model (route co-design + FSTP for SME twin transition + branding + replicability), and link the concept directly to the needs identified in 1.1-1.2.
criteria.example_weak: Describing the project as "promoting cooperation and learning in tourism" without defining the route as the structuring concept.
criteria.example_strong: The project follows a route ecosystem model where a co-designed transnational thematic route serves as the operational backbone, mobilising a structured network of tourism SMEs supported through a transparent FSTP scheme, integrated with branding, governance and replicability components, to deliver measurable twin transition and territorial cohesion outcomes.
criteria.avoid: Avoid defining the concept through activities alone. Avoid abstract notions like "innovation" or "cooperation" without operational substance. Avoid omitting the route as the structuring concept.

criteria.title: Multi-level governance proportionate to the route ecosystem project
criteria.intent: To ensure a clear governance system adapted to a transnational route ecosystem project with FSTP delivery.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe: the Coordinator (overall coordination, EISMEA reporting, financial oversight); the Steering Committee (all 6 partners, quarterly meetings, strategic decisions); WP leaders (operational coordination of each of the 4 mandatory WPs); the FSTP Independent Evaluation Committee (SME selection with conflict of interest declarations); implementation teams within each partner organisation.
criteria.example_weak: Saying only that the coordinator manages the project.
criteria.example_strong: Governance has 4 levels: Coordinator (Permacultura Cantabria) for overall coordination and EISMEA interface; Steering Committee of all 6 partners meeting quarterly (online) and biannually (in-person) for strategic decisions; WP leaders for operational coordination of each of the 4 mandatory WPs; FSTP Independent Evaluation Committee selecting SMEs with conflict of interest declarations published.
criteria.avoid: Avoid overly complex governance structures. Avoid flat structures without defined coordination roles.

criteria.title: Continuation of skills, knowledge and SME competitiveness gains
criteria.intent: To explain how skills, knowledge and SME competitiveness gains developed during the project continue to be used after it ends.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe: continued use of the SME twin transition improvements (digital tools, sustainability practices, accessibility upgrades) funded through FSTP; continued operation of the post-FSTP SMEs as nodes of the route; continued application of the methodologies by DMOs and BSOs in their ordinary activities; continued use of the toolkit by other European territories.
criteria.example_weak: Participants will keep using their skills.
criteria.example_strong: Each of the 30 FSTP-supported SMEs continues to operate the improvements funded (digital booking, accessibility, energy efficiency), with the platform providing ongoing visitor flow that sustains the investment; DMOs and BSOs integrate the route management and FSTP delivery methodologies into their ordinary activities; the toolkit is replicated by 10+ European territories.
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague statements. Avoid assuming automatic continuation without explanation.

criteria.title: Balanced and complementary project team
criteria.intent: To demonstrate that the consortium has balanced human resources covering all functions: management, tourism/route expertise, FSTP design and delivery, capacity building, communication, monitoring.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe the team composition with complementary profiles across the 6 partner organisations, ensuring full coverage of all WP functions without overlap. It must show that DMOs bring tourism and route expertise, BSOs bring SME support and FSTP delivery expertise, and the coordinator brings overall management capacity. It must list profiles aligned with SMP budget category A.
criteria.example_weak: Listing staff without explaining complementarity.
criteria.example_strong: The team combines a project manager (coordinator, category A.1), 2 senior tourism experts (DMOs, A.1), 2 senior SME support experts with FSTP experience (BSOs, A.1), 1 financial officer, 1 communication officer, 1 monitoring and evaluation specialist, and junior support staff, ensuring full coverage of route design, FSTP delivery, communication and evaluation functions.
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting the team as a simple list. Avoid overlap between roles.

criteria.title: Transparent SMP-compliant financial management system
criteria.intent: To ensure a clear, structured, transparent financial management system fully compliant with SMP Annotated Grant Agreement requirements.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe: the coordinator's central role in financial coordination and EISMEA reporting; each partner's responsibility for its own budget under SMP-AGA rules; standardised cost reporting templates; reporting cycles (continuous through the EISMEA Portal, interim at midterm, final at M36+60 days); audit trail maintenance for 5 years post-final payment.
criteria.example_weak: Saying financial management will be transparent without explaining how.
