criteria.example_strong: The coordinator centralises financial coordination and EISMEA reporting; each partner manages its own budget through standardised SMP-AGA-compliant templates; cost reports are consolidated quarterly internally and submitted to EISMEA at midterm and final stages; documentary evidence is retained for 5 years post-final payment.
criteria.avoid: Avoid generic transparency statements. NEVER reference EACEA or Erasmus+ financial rules — this project is SMP-AGA / EISMEA.

criteria.title: Concrete transformation for tourism SMEs and the route ecosystem
criteria.intent: To ensure the project clearly explains what changes for each target group in practical, measurable terms.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe transformation by target group: (a) tourism SMEs — adoption of digital tools, sustainability practices, accessibility upgrades, integration into route offer, increased competitiveness, jobs created/maintained; (b) DMOs and BSOs — strengthened capacity for transnational route management and FSTP delivery; (c) wider ecosystem stakeholders — engaged in route governance, intangible heritage valorised; (d) local communities — increased tourism flows to under-visited territories, reduced over-tourism in saturated cores, economic benefits, well-being; (e) European tourism sector — validated route model and toolkit available for replication.
criteria.example_weak: SMEs will improve sustainability.
criteria.example_strong: 30 tourism SMEs along the route will adopt at least one digital tool and one sustainability practice through FSTP, generating an estimated 60-90 jobs created/maintained; 2 DMOs will integrate transnational route management methodologies; 12 ecosystem stakeholders will engage in route governance through Letters of Intent and joint actions; under-visited territories (Pasiegos, Béarn interior) will see +20% tourism flow during shoulder seasons; the validated route toolkit will be available for replication in 10+ European territories.
criteria.avoid: Avoid generic statements. Avoid mixing all target groups in one sentence. Avoid repeating objectives instead of describing transformation.

criteria.title: Coordination treated as transversal function — never as WP5
criteria.intent: Coordination is a transversal management function carried out by the Coordinator across all 4 mandatory WPs. The call does NOT establish coordination as a separate WP. Coordination tasks and costs are typically distributed within the 4 mandatory WPs or covered through dedicated coordination effort within the budget.
criteria.elements: Coordination functions include: overall project management; financial coordination and EISMEA reporting; Steering Committee organisation; Consortium Agreement management; risk register maintenance; quality assurance across all WPs; FSTP scheme oversight (without conflict of interest — the FSTP evaluation committee is independent); communication with EISMEA; deliverable consolidation and submission. In the work plan narrative, these functions should be described under the Coordinator's role across all WPs, not as a separate WP5. If the Application Form structurally requires a dedicated coordination/management section, it should be framed as Project Management within the proposal (separate from the 4 mandatory WPs) rather than as WP5.
criteria.example_weak: Creating a WP5 "Coordination and Project Management" that displaces the call's mandatory 4-WP structure.
criteria.example_strong: Treating coordination as a transversal function described across all WPs and in the management section, fully respecting the call's mandatory 4-WP structure.
criteria.avoid: NEVER create a 5th WP for coordination — the call imposes 4 mandatory thematic WPs. Coordination is transversal.

criteria.title: Tailored communication for tourism route ecosystem audiences
criteria.intent: To ensure communication is adapted to different audiences within the tourism route ecosystem.
criteria.elements: Target groups must be addressed separately. Tourism SMEs along the route receive practical, actionable content (route guidelines, FSTP information, toolkits, training). DMOs and BSOs across Europe receive methodological resources and replicable tools. Policymakers receive policy briefs and impact data. Travellers and general public receive the route narrative, multimedia content, and booking access. Each message must be adapted in tone, depth and format.
criteria.example_weak: We will share results with stakeholders.
criteria.example_strong: Tourism SMEs receive practical FSTP application kits and post-implementation toolkits; DMOs and BSOs receive the replicability toolkit published on the EU Tourism Platform; policymakers receive 2 policy briefs aligned with the European Agenda for Tourism 2030; travellers access multilingual route content via the SUSTRAI platform, partner DMO portals, and the Visit Europe portal.
criteria.avoid: Avoid treating all audiences the same. Avoid generic communication. Avoid omitting policymaker audience (critical for replicability).

criteria.title: Structured project management and governance under SMP
criteria.intent: To ensure the project has a clear, structured and functional governance system enabling transnational coordination under SMP rules.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe: the role of the coordinator (legal representative, financial coordinator, EISMEA liaison); the Steering Committee composed of all 6 partners; WP leaders (DMOs leading territorial WPs, BSOs leading FSTP); decision-making rules; communication channels (monthly online meetings, biannual in-person meetings rotating between countries, shared collaboration platform); the Consortium Agreement signed before project start; alignment with SMP Annotated Grant Agreement obligations.
criteria.example_weak: Stating that the coordinator manages the project without explaining the governance structure.
criteria.example_strong: The project is coordinated by Permacultura Cantabria with a Steering Committee of all 6 partners meeting quarterly online and biannually in person (rotating between Spain and France), WP leaders responsible for operational delivery (DMOs lead route WPs, BSOs lead FSTP), and consensus-based strategic decisions with qualified majority fallback codified in the Consortium Agreement signed at M1.
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague management language. Avoid reference to Erasmus+ or EACEA — this project is SMP/EISMEA. Avoid presenting coordination as a separate WP.

criteria.title: Strategic consortium composition compliant with mandatory eligibility
criteria.intent: To demonstrate that the consortium composition is intentional, route-driven, and fully compliant with the mandatory eligibility criteria of the call.
criteria.elements: The answer must explicitly confirm: at least 3 DMOs from at least 2 different eligible countries; at least 2 BSOs from at least 2 different eligible countries; total of 5-8 entities. It must identify each partner with name, country, type (DMO or BSO), and rationale for inclusion (territorial coverage, expertise, complementarity). It must justify the geographical distribution along the proposed route.
criteria.example_weak: Listing partners without confirming DMO/BSO categories and country distribution.
criteria.example_strong: The consortium comprises 6 entities: 3 DMOs (Agencia Valles Pasiegos / ES; DMO País Vasco / ES; French Tourism Office / FR) and 3 BSOs (Permacultura Cantabria / ES as coordinator; Spanish Chamber of Commerce / ES; French Chamber of Commerce / FR), fully meeting the 3 DMOs in 2+ countries and 2 BSOs in 2+ countries criteria, with balanced territorial coverage along the transnational route.
criteria.avoid: Avoid describing tourism SMEs as consortium partners. Avoid omitting confirmation of the mandatory eligibility criteria. Avoid presenting partner selection as opportunistic.

criteria.title: Operational planning and implementation control
criteria.intent: To ensure structured mechanisms to plan, track and control implementation.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe how implementation is planned (Gantt chart with milestones M1-M36, deliverables tracker), tracked (monthly progress reports from WP leaders, quarterly Steering Committee reviews), and controlled (deviation alerts, corrective action protocols, financial monitoring against budget). It must explain integration with the SMP reporting cycle (interim report at midterm, final report at M36).
criteria.example_weak: Stating that the project will follow a timeline without explaining tracking and control.
criteria.example_strong: The project is planned through a Gantt with monthly milestones and quarterly Steering Committee reviews; WP leaders submit monthly progress reports through a shared platform; deviations trigger corrective action protocols within 15 days; financial monitoring is integrated with operational tracking and consolidated for the SMP interim report at M18 and final report at M36.
criteria.avoid: Avoid static planning. Avoid assuming implementation follows the plan without active control.

criteria.title: Work package linkage
criteria.intent: To ensure each risk is clearly linked to a specific WP, demonstrating risk management is embedded in implementation.
criteria.elements: Each risk must specify the most affected WP from the 4 mandatory WPs: WP1 (route establishment), WP2.1 (capacity building), WP2.2 (FSTP), WP3 (communication), WP4 (best practices). If a risk spans multiple WPs, indicate the one with highest impact.
criteria.example_weak: Affects the project.
criteria.example_strong: WP2.2 — Financial Support to Third Parties (FSTP).
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague references like "all WPs" or "the project".

criteria.title: Clear roles and task distribution aligned with WPs
criteria.intent: To ensure each profile has clearly defined responsibilities linked to specific WPs and tasks.
criteria.elements: The answer must explicitly assign each staff profile to specific WPs and tasks: project manager (transversal coordination), DMO senior experts (lead WP1 and WP3), BSO senior experts (lead WP2.1 and WP2.2), monitoring specialist (transversal monitoring), communication officer (WP3), replicability specialist (WP4). Each role must be linked to actual project deliverables.
criteria.example_weak: Mentioning roles without linking to actual tasks.
criteria.example_strong: The project manager (Permacultura Cantabria, 0.5 FTE) leads transversal coordination and EISMEA reporting; the senior tourism experts at Agencia Valles Pasiegos and the French DMO (0.4 FTE each) lead WP1 route co-design; the BSO senior experts at the two Chambers of Commerce (0.5 FTE each) lead WP2.2 FSTP scheme.
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague roles. Avoid disconnection from actual project activities.

criteria.title: WP2.1 — Engagement and capacity building (within WP2)
criteria.intent: WP2.1 mobilises and capacitates the SME ecosystem of the route through outreach, training and mentoring — without direct funding to participants (which is the role of WP2.2). It is the engagement and learning layer that makes the FSTP scheme effective.
criteria.elements: WP2.1 typically runs M6-M30. Tasks: T2.1.1 SME outreach and engagement strategy (through Chambers, DMOs, associations, EEN); T2.1.2 design of capacity-building curriculum aligned with the Pact for Skills and PANTOUR (covering sustainability, digital, accessibility, business model, marketing, crisis management); T2.1.3 training delivery (workshops, online modules, study visits, peer exchanges); T2.1.4 mentoring of FSTP recipients (ongoing throughout WP2.2); T2.1.5 capacity building for local authorities and DMOs on adaptive destination management; T2.1.6 youth and community engagement (linkage with intangible heritage). Deliverables WP2.1: D2.1.1 SME engagement and outreach strategy; D2.1.2 Capacity-building curriculum aligned with Pact for Skills/PANTOUR; D2.1.3 Training delivery reports (cumulative); D2.1.4 Mentoring reports for FSTP recipients; D2.1.5 Authorities/DMO capacity-building report; D2.1.6 Youth and community engagement report. Milestones: 100+ SMEs engaged by M12; full curriculum delivered by M24; mentoring completed for all FSTP recipients by M30.
criteria.example_weak: Mixing engagement/training and FSTP cascade funding into one undifferentiated WP2.
criteria.example_strong: Clear separation between WP2.1 (engagement, outreach, curriculum, training, mentoring — no funding to participants) and WP2.2 (FSTP cascade funding), with curriculum aligned to Pact for Skills/PANTOUR and mentoring linked to FSTP recipients.
criteria.avoid: Do NOT confuse WP2.1 (no direct funding) with WP2.2 (FSTP). Do NOT omit the curriculum alignment with Pact for Skills/PANTOUR. Do NOT skip mentoring of FSTP recipients.

criteria.title: Multi-channel dissemination aligned with Destination Europe framework
criteria.intent: To ensure dissemination uses appropriate, diverse channels aligned with the call's mandatory framework.
criteria.elements: Channels must include: digital (project website ES/FR/EN/EU, social media, newsletters, video content); institutional (partner networks, Chamber networks, DMO networks, tourism associations); events (workshops, multiplier events, sectoral conferences, the mandatory Brussels event); direct SME engagement (FSTP information sessions, training, mentoring). Alignment with Destination Europe framework, Visit Europe portal and national portals (visit France, Spain.info) is mandatory and must be operationalised.
criteria.example_weak: We will use social media and emails.
criteria.example_strong: Channels: multilingual project website (ES/FR/EN/EU); social media (LinkedIn for institutional audiences, Instagram and TikTok for traveller and SME audiences); 2 mandatory short videos (1-2 min, one at M3 and one at M30); 6 multiplier events (3 per country); the mandatory Brussels event (2 days, 5 people); content syndicated to Visit Europe portal and national portals (visit France, Spain.info); EU Tourism Platform publications.
criteria.avoid: Avoid listing channels without explanation. Avoid omitting the Destination Europe framework. Avoid omitting the mandatory 2 videos and Brussels event.

criteria.title: Cost-effective use of resources concentrated on SME impact
criteria.intent: To ensure financial resources are used efficiently and concentrated on activities delivering maximum impact for tourism SMEs and the route ecosystem.
criteria.elements: The answer must explain: concentration of significant budget share on FSTP (~27% directly to SMEs at 100% co-financing); leveraging existing partner infrastructure (offices, digital platforms, networks) to avoid duplication; use of hybrid/online working modalities to reduce travel costs while preserving essential in-person coordination; shared tools and templates across partners.
criteria.example_weak: Stating the project is cost-effective without explaining how.
criteria.example_strong: Approximately 27% of the budget is dedicated to the FSTP (lump sums of 7,000-15,000 € to 30 tourism SMEs at 100% co-financing), maximising direct impact; partners leverage existing infrastructure and networks; hybrid working reduces travel costs while preserving biannual in-person meetings critical for cross-border trust; shared digital platforms are used across the consortium.
criteria.avoid: Avoid generic claims of efficiency. Avoid listing cost-saving measures without linking to project outputs.

criteria.title: Methodological coherence and route-specific approach
criteria.intent: To ensure the methodology is appropriate for a route ecosystem intervention and capable of achieving the project's objectives.
criteria.elements: The answer must explain the methodology: participatory co-design with stakeholders for route definition (WP1), open and competitive FSTP for SMEs (WP2.2), capacity building and mentoring (WP2.1), strategic communication aligned with Destination Europe framework (WP3), peer learning and replicability (WP4). It must explain why each methodological choice is appropriate for the route and the SME ecosystem.
criteria.example_weak: Mentioning training and dissemination without explaining how they support the route ecosystem.
criteria.example_strong: The methodology combines participatory route co-design with regional stakeholders, an open and transparent FSTP scheme selecting 30 tourism SMEs through competitive criteria, mentoring on twin transition adoption, and integrated communication aligned with the Destination Europe framework, ensuring that the route is co-owned by its ecosystem and that SME transformation is structurally embedded.
criteria.avoid: Avoid listing methods without explanation. Avoid methodologies disconnected from the route concept.

criteria.title: Data-based justification of the need
criteria.intent: To prove that the problem is real, measurable, and recognised at EU level by policy bodies and statistical sources.
criteria.elements: The answer must include EU-level statistics and references from sources such as the European Commission (DG GROW), Eurostat tourism statistics, the EU Tourism Dashboard, the Transition Pathway for Tourism, OECD Tourism Trends and Policies, and UNWTO reports. It must explain what the data means in concrete terms for tourism SMEs and the route ecosystem.
criteria.example_weak: Studies show SMEs face sustainability challenges.
criteria.example_strong: According to the European Commission's Transition Pathway for Tourism (2022), over 90% of tourism businesses in the EU are SMEs, yet a significant proportion lack the operational capacity to adopt circular and digital practices, limiting their ability to respond to the growing demand for sustainable travel identified by the EU Tourism Dashboard.
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague references, data without explanation, or unrelated statistics. Do not cite irrelevant frameworks; reference only those directly applicable to tourism SME competitiveness and the twin transition.

criteria.title: Post-project governance entity for the route ecosystem
criteria.intent: To show how cross-border cooperation between partners and the route brand continue through a legal post-project entity.
criteria.elements: The answer must evaluate three legal options for the post-project entity: (a) transnational not-for-profit association registered in one of the two countries; (b) EGTC (European Grouping of Territorial Cooperation) — particularly attractive for EISMEA cross-border projects, public-private legal personality recognised across the EU; (c) AEIE (European Economic Interest Grouping) — if the economic-commercial dimension dominates. The entity is designed in WP1, constituted in the last 12 months of the project, and operational at M36, owning the brand, the platform, the quality standards, and the SME network coordination function.
criteria.example_weak: Partners will stay in contact.
criteria.example_strong: A post-project entity (preferred legal form: EGTC, with the alternative of a transnational not-for-profit association) is designed during WP1, constituted at M30, and operational at M36; it owns the SUSTRAI brand, the digital platform, the quality standards, and coordinates the SME network beyond the funded period, with formal statutes, governance bodies and audited accounts.
criteria.avoid: Avoid informal continuity claims. Avoid omitting the legal entity. Avoid the unproven assumption that networks continue without structure.

criteria.title: Complementarity DMO-BSO and European added value
criteria.intent: To demonstrate that DMOs and BSOs bring complementary expertise from across the tourism route value chain.
criteria.elements: The answer must explain: DMOs contribute tourism strategy, route design, territorial coordination, stakeholder mobilisation, and dissemination through tourism networks (Destination Europe framework, national portals); BSOs contribute SME outreach (Chambers' direct SME networks), FSTP design and delivery, mentoring, capacity building, and access to business support infrastructure. Cross-border testing (ES + FR) enables comparison, adaptation and transferability of the route model.
criteria.example_weak: Stating that DMOs and BSOs complement each other without explaining how.
criteria.example_strong: DMOs bring tourism route design and territorial coordination expertise, mobilising tourism stakeholders through their established networks and aligning with the Destination Europe framework; BSOs (notably the Chambers of Commerce) bring direct access to thousands of SMEs through their statutory networks, FSTP delivery experience, and capacity-building infrastructure, creating a full-chain DMO-to-SME pipeline that no single category could deliver alone.
criteria.avoid: Avoid generic complementarity claims. Avoid omitting the European cross-border dimension.

criteria.title: Transparent decision-making and Consortium Agreement
criteria.intent: To ensure decisions are taken in a transparent, efficient way and codified in the Consortium Agreement.
criteria.elements: The answer must explain: operational decisions by WP leaders; strategic decisions by the Steering Committee through consensus, with qualified majority (4 out of 6) fallback; conflict resolution and escalation to the Coordinator; the Consortium Agreement signed by M1 codifying decision rules, IPR, financial flows, and confidentiality, in line with the SMP-AGA Article 7.
criteria.example_weak: Decisions will be taken jointly.
criteria.example_strong: Operational decisions are taken by WP leaders within their scope; strategic decisions by the Steering Committee through consensus, with qualified majority (4 out of 6) as fallback; conflicts escalate to the Coordinator and, if unresolved, to mediation per the Consortium Agreement signed at M1, codifying SMP-AGA Article 7 obligations on internal arrangements.
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague decision rules. Avoid missing the Consortium Agreement. Avoid referencing inappropriate legal frameworks.

criteria.title: Methodological and operational innovation in route ecosystem design
criteria.intent: To demonstrate that the project introduces clear added value compared to existing route or SME support practices.
criteria.elements: The answer must define concretely what is new or improved: an integrated route-ecosystem model combining FSTP, governance and branding; new approaches to cross-border SME engagement; integration of digital tools (smart tourism data, AR for intangible heritage, federated booking systems) into rural and regenerative tourism; novel post-project governance models (EGTC, AEIE, transnational association); integration of the European Accessibility Act compliance from project design.
criteria.example_weak: Stating that the project is innovative without explaining what is new or how it improves on existing practices.
criteria.example_strong: The project introduces an integrated route-ecosystem model combining a transparent FSTP scheme for 30 SMEs, a digital platform with AR-based valorisation of intangible heritage, and a co-designed post-project governance structure (an EGTC), going beyond conventional route-marketing approaches to deliver structural, auto-sustainable transformation of the tourism ecosystem.
criteria.avoid: Avoid using the word "innovation" without explanation. Avoid presenting standard activities as innovative.

criteria.title: Multi-level needs (route SMEs as FSTP recipients + partner organisations)
criteria.intent: To ensure the needs analysis considers both the needs of tourism SMEs (the target group, FSTP recipients) and the needs of participating organisations (DMOs and BSOs as consortium partners), demonstrating that the project responds to ecosystem challenges at multiple levels.
criteria.elements: The answer must clearly describe the needs of tourism SMEs along the route (access to applicable sustainability tools, digital upgrades, integration into structured route offers, accessibility compliance under the European Accessibility Act, skills development aligned with the Pact for Skills and PANTOUR). It must then describe the specific needs of each partner organisation. DMOs need updated methodologies for transnational route management, integration with Destination Europe framework, and capacity for cross-border ecosystem coordination. BSOs need to strengthen their capacity to design and operate FSTP schemes compliant with EU rules, deliver tailored SME support, and engage in transnational cooperation. Each partner's needs must be linked to their role in the project. Tourism SMEs are FSTP recipients, NOT partners.
criteria.example_weak: Describing general SME needs without distinguishing between SMEs (as targets), DMOs (as ecosystem coordinators) and BSOs (as SME support agents), or describing partners generically.
criteria.example_strong: Explaining that tourism SMEs along the route lack applicable sustainability and digital tools and access to coordinated support; that DMOs need updated transnational route management methodologies aligned with the Destination Europe framework; and that BSOs need to strengthen their FSTP design and delivery capacity to provide tailored support compliant with EU rules.
criteria.avoid: Avoid treating all stakeholders as having the same needs. Avoid describing tourism SMEs as consortium partners (they are FSTP recipients). Avoid describing partners generically without linking their needs to their consortium role.

criteria.title: Short, medium and long-term impact pathway
criteria.intent: To ensure impact is articulated as a transformation process across time.
criteria.elements: The answer must structure impact in three layers. Short-term (M1-M18, during implementation): route co-designed and mapped; FSTP open call launched and SMEs selected; capacity building underway; baseline established. Medium-term (M18-M36, application phase): SMEs implementing improvements; route operational with branding deployed; tourism flows being redistributed; post-project governance designed and constituted. Long-term (M36+, systemic): auto-sustained route maintained by post-project entity; SME competitiveness consolidated; twin transition embedded; intangible heritage valorised durably; toolkit replicated in other European territories; structural integration of the route into national tourism strategies.
criteria.example_weak: Mixing learning and long-term change in one paragraph.
criteria.example_strong: Short-term (M1-M18): route co-designed, FSTP launched, 30 SMEs selected. Medium-term (M18-M36): SMEs applying improvements, route operational with deployed branding, post-project EGTC constituted. Long-term (M36+): the route is auto-sustained by the EGTC through SME fees, booking commissions and tour operator partnerships; the toolkit is replicated in 10+ European territories.
criteria.avoid: Avoid unclear timelines. Avoid repeating the same result across stages. Avoid describing activities instead of outcomes.

criteria.title: Clear partner roles linked to mandatory WPs
criteria.intent: To ensure each partner has a clearly justified role aligned with the call's mandatory 4-WP structure.
criteria.elements: The answer must assign each partner to specific WP leadership and contribution roles: a DMO leads WP1 (route co-design); BSOs lead WP2.1 (capacity building) and WP2.2 (FSTP); a DMO leads WP3 (communication/branding); the coordinator leads WP4 (best practices/replicability) and transversal coordination. Each role must be linked to demonstrated expertise.
criteria.example_weak: Saying all partners contribute equally to all WPs.
criteria.example_strong: WP1 is led by the French DMO (with co-leadership from Agencia Valles Pasiegos for the Spanish side), bringing route co-design expertise; WP2.1 is led by the Spanish Chamber and WP2.2 by the French Chamber, both with FSTP experience; WP3 is led by the Basque DMO; WP4 is led by the coordinator (Permacultura Cantabria) through its EU Funding School platform.
criteria.avoid: Avoid identical roles for all partners. Avoid disconnection from the WP structure. Avoid assigning WPs to partners without expertise alignment.

criteria.title: Rationale for intervention and need for transnational cooperation
criteria.intent: To explain why action is needed now and why a transnational route-based project is the right type of solution.
criteria.elements: The answer must explain why the problem is urgent in the current context (twin transition deadlines, competitiveness pressure, climate adaptation, demographic decline of rural areas), what happens if nothing is done (loss of SME competitiveness, degradation of destinations, unsustainable tourism patterns, accelerated depopulation), why SMEs cannot solve this alone (fragmentation, limited scale, lack of access to shared tools and methodologies), and why transnational cooperation is essential (route-based interventions require cross-border coordination, solutions need to be transferable and scalable, peer learning between destinations improves quality).
criteria.example_weak: This issue is important and needs a project.
criteria.example_strong: The fragmented structure of the tourism SME sector, combined with limited access to resources and the absence of shared methodologies for route-based offers, makes it impossible for individual businesses to implement competitiveness transformation alone, justifying a transnational cooperation framework that enables co-design, peer learning, and replication across European destinations.
criteria.avoid: Avoid saying "a project is needed" without justification. Avoid weak justification of cooperation based on mere geographic diversity. Avoid presenting the route as optional — it is the structuring concept.

criteria.title: Complementarity with EU initiatives and networks
criteria.intent: To demonstrate that the project does not duplicate existing EU initiatives but rather connects to and amplifies them.
criteria.elements: The answer must explicitly mention complementarity with relevant EU initiatives and platforms: Destination Europe framework, EU Tourism Platform, Enterprise Europe Network, European Digital Innovation Hubs, European Cluster Collaboration Platform, Pact for Skills (Tourism partnership), PANTOUR, Interreg programmes covering the route territories. It must explain how the project leverages, feeds into, or complements each. Strict prohibition of double funding must be respected.
criteria.example_weak: Mentioning EU initiatives without explaining how the project connects to them.
criteria.example_strong: The project will publish its toolkit and best practices on the EU Tourism Platform (mandatory under WP4), coordinate dissemination with the Destination Europe framework, mobilise Enterprise Europe Network nodes in both countries to extend SME outreach, and engage European Digital Innovation Hubs to support FSTP recipients with technical advice on digital adoption.
criteria.avoid: Avoid generic claims of complementarity. Avoid double-funding risk (the same activities cannot be financed twice from EU sources).

criteria.title: Structured multilingual communication framework
criteria.intent: To ensure continuous alignment between partners through structured communication tools.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe: monthly online coordination calls per WP; quarterly Steering Committee meetings online; biannual in-person meetings rotating between Spain and France; shared digital platform (project management tool, document repository, version control); structured reporting templates; English as the consortium working language with multilingual materials (ES/FR/EU) for SME-facing outputs.
criteria.example_weak: Partners will communicate regularly.
criteria.example_strong: Communication architecture: monthly online WP coordination calls; quarterly Steering Committee meetings online; biannual in-person meetings (rotating between Spanish and French partner sites); shared platform (SharePoint/Teams) for documents, tasks and decisions; English as working language with multilingual SME-facing materials.
criteria.avoid: Avoid generic statements. Avoid omitting language coordination protocols.

criteria.title: WP2.2 — Financial Support to Third Parties (FSTP) — MANDATORY
criteria.intent: WP2.2 is MANDATORY and is the most strictly regulated WP of the call. It delivers the cascade funding scheme to 10-30 tourism SMEs (lump sums 7,000-15,000 €) and must comply with EU FSTP good practice guidance (open transparent call, independent evaluation, conflict of interest, double-funding declarations, audit trail).
criteria.elements: WP2.2 typically runs M9-M30. Tasks must include: T2.2.1 design of the FSTP scheme (eligibility criteria, evaluation methodology, conflict of interest rules) — published before the open call; T2.2.2 launch of the open call (minimum 2-month open period, public, transparent, competitive); T2.2.3 SME applicant outreach in coordination with Chambers, DMOs, EEN; T2.2.4 independent evaluation committee with conflict of interest declarations and published criteria; T2.2.5 selection of 10-30 SMEs (target: 30) across three tiers (7,000 € basic / 10,000 € intermediate / 15,000 € advanced); T2.2.6 signature of FSTP sub-grant agreements with mandatory double-funding declarations at start and end; T2.2.7 milestone-based lump-sum payments to SMEs; T2.2.8 monitoring of SME implementation (technical and financial); T2.2.9 publication of FSTP results on the EU Tourism Platform and partner websites; T2.2.10 audit-ready documentation maintained for 5 years post-final payment. Deliverables WP2.2: D2.2.1 FSTP scheme design document with published criteria; D2.2.2 Open call publication (web + Chamber/DMO networks); D2.2.3 Evaluation committee report with conflict of interest declarations; D2.2.4 List of selected SMEs and signed sub-grant agreements; D2.2.5 FSTP implementation progress reports; D2.2.6 Final FSTP impact report (number of SMEs supported, lump sums disbursed, results achieved). Milestones: FSTP scheme published (M9); FSTP open call launched (M9-M12 minimum 2 months); SMEs selected (M12); first payments disbursed (M13); midterm FSTP review (M21); FSTP closure (M30).
criteria.example_weak: WP2 described as generic "capacity building" without FSTP, without compliance with EU FSTP good practices, without conflict of interest rules, without tiered structure.
criteria.example_strong: WP2 with clear separation of WP2.1 (engagement, no funding) and WP2.2 (FSTP, mandatory cascade funding), full compliance with EU FSTP good practices, independent evaluation committee, published criteria, tiered structure (7k/10k/15k), milestone-based payments, double-funding declarations, EU Tourism Platform publication.
criteria.avoid: NEVER omit the FSTP — it is mandatory. NEVER skip conflict of interest declarations. NEVER omit the minimum 2-month open call period. NEVER present FSTP recipients as direct EU funding beneficiaries — they are "co-funded by the EU". NEVER omit the audit trail requirement.

criteria.title: Long-term accessibility of project results
criteria.intent: To ensure project results remain accessible and usable after the project ends.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe: the post-project entity hosting the digital platform and content (≥ 5 years post-project); the toolkit published on the EU Tourism Platform (permanent EU repository); partner websites hosting project results (≥ 5 years per SMP-AGA Article 22); open-access licensing for the toolkit (Creative Commons or equivalent).
criteria.example_weak: Results will be shared online.
criteria.example_strong: The digital platform is hosted by the post-project entity for at least 5 years post-M36; the replicability toolkit is published on the EU Tourism Platform (permanent EU repository), under Creative Commons BY 4.0 license; partner websites archive project results for ≥ 5 years per SMP-AGA Article 22.
criteria.avoid: Avoid temporary dissemination. Avoid omitting long-term maintenance arrangements.

criteria.title: Demonstrated FSTP and tourism ecosystem experience
criteria.intent: To ensure the team has the specific expertise required for SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME, particularly FSTP delivery and tourism ecosystem coordination.
criteria.elements: The answer must highlight: experience in EU-funded projects (with concrete references); experience in FSTP / cascade funding design and delivery (critical for the BSOs leading WP2.2); experience in tourism route management and DMO operations; expertise in twin transition (sustainability + digital); experience in cross-border cooperation.
criteria.example_weak: Stating the team is experienced without specifying areas.
criteria.example_strong: BSO senior experts at the lead Chambers of Commerce have prior experience in COSME-funded cascade funding schemes including [project acronyms], delivering FSTP to over 100 SMEs cumulatively; DMO experts have managed Interreg POCTEFA cross-border tourism projects; sustainability experts at the coordinator hold credentials in regenerative tourism and circular economy.
criteria.avoid: Avoid generic statements. Avoid omitting FSTP-specific experience for BSO staff (this is critical for the call).

criteria.title: Translation of needs into SMART objectives
criteria.intent: To ensure that all project objectives derive directly from identified needs and describe concrete changes the project aims to achieve, not activities.
criteria.elements: Each objective must clearly respond to a specific need and describe what will improve or change as a result of the project. Objectives must be expressed as outcomes (not activities), be realistic within the 30-48 month project timeframe, and focus on concrete change — increased SME capacity to adopt sustainable practices, strengthened route ecosystem governance, enhanced organisational capabilities for cross-border cooperation, redistribution of tourism flows from over-visited to under-visited territories, valorisation of intangible heritage. Objectives must be drafted using SMART logic.
criteria.example_weak: Describing objectives in terms of activities such as "organise workshops" or "create a website", without expressing the expected change.
criteria.example_strong: To improve the capacity of at least 30 tourism SMEs along the route to implement sustainable and digital practices, through targeted support and integration into a structured transnational route ecosystem, leading to measurable competitiveness gains and integration of intangible heritage trades.
criteria.avoid: Avoid writing activities instead of outcomes. Avoid combining multiple unrelated outcomes in a single objective. Avoid vague language. Avoid objectives that cannot be measured.

criteria.title: Activity sequence aligned with mandatory WP structure
criteria.intent: To ensure project activities are organised in a logical and progressive sequence reflecting the call's mandatory 4-WP structure.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe how the project unfolds across 36 months through the 4 mandatory WPs: WP1 (M1-M9 route co-design and ecosystem mapping), WP2 (M6-M30 capacity building + FSTP open call, selection and implementation), WP3 (M3-M36 continuous communication and branding), WP4 (M18-M36 best practices, replicability, and EU Tourism Platform publication). Coordination is a transversal management function, not a separate WP.
criteria.example_weak: Describing WPs separately without showing temporal articulation, or proposing a WP structure that does not match the call's mandatory 4-WP architecture.
criteria.example_strong: The project begins with route co-design and ecosystem mapping (WP1, M1-M9), feeding into the FSTP open call (WP2.2, M9-M12) and the capacity building cycle (WP2.1, M6-M30), with continuous communication and branding (WP3, M3-M36) and a final replicability phase (WP4, M18-M36) producing the toolkit published on the EU Tourism Platform.
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting WPs as isolated. Avoid creating WPs that do not match the call's mandatory structure. Avoid treating coordination as a separate WP.

criteria.title: Systematic quality assurance across the route ecosystem
criteria.intent: To ensure quality is continuously monitored and improved across the route, the FSTP, and all deliverables.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe internal review of all deliverables before EISMEA submission, peer validation between partners, feedback collection from FSTP recipients (mandatory mid-project and end-of-project feedback), testing of communication outputs with target audiences, and continuous improvement cycles. Quality assurance must extend to FSTP processes: independent evaluation of FSTP applicants, transparent scoring, conflict of interest declarations.
criteria.example_weak: Saying "quality will be ensured" without explaining the mechanisms.
criteria.example_strong: All deliverables are reviewed by a designated Quality Manager, peer-validated by at least two partners before EISMEA submission, and (where applicable) tested with FSTP recipients before finalisation; FSTP applications are scored by an independent evaluation committee with conflict of interest declarations and published criteria, in line with EU FSTP good practice guidance.
criteria.avoid: Avoid treating quality as a general principle. Avoid limiting quality control to the final stage.

criteria.title: Quantifiable indicators aligned with EU Tourism Dashboard
criteria.intent: To ensure impact is measurable, evidence-based and aggregatable at European level.
criteria.elements: The answer must list indicators by EU Tourism Dashboard category: Economic (number of SMEs supported, jobs created/maintained, % SME revenue growth); Environmental (% SMEs with eco-certification, t CO₂ saved, % SMEs applying circular practices); Social (number of stakeholders engaged, accessibility improvements, intangible heritage activities); Digital (% SMEs with professional digital presence, % SMEs integrated into federated booking system, platform users); Governance (post-project entity constituted, KPI dashboard public). Each indicator must include unit, baseline and target.
criteria.example_weak: Number of participants involved.
criteria.example_strong: 60% of the 30 FSTP-supported SMEs (target: 18 SMEs; baseline: 0) implement at least one sustainability practice within 12 months of FSTP completion, mapped to EU Tourism Dashboard category 'Environmental Sustainability'.
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague indicators. Avoid counting only participation. Avoid omitting baseline/target/unit.

criteria.title: Likelihood and impact rating after mitigation
criteria.intent: To ensure each risk is realistically assessed in terms of probability and severity, including AFTER mitigation measures are considered.
criteria.elements: Each risk must include: likelihood (low/medium/high), impact (low/medium/high), with a brief justification of the rating in the route ecosystem context. The rating must reflect the residual risk after mitigation, not the gross risk.
criteria.example_weak: Assigning generic values without justification.
criteria.example_strong: Likelihood: medium (because SMEs in micro-enterprise format face time constraints during high season) / Impact: high (would compromise the achievement of the 30-SME target and undermine WP2.2 outcomes) — after mitigation: likelihood medium, impact medium.
criteria.avoid: Avoid assigning ratings without justification. Avoid omitting either likelihood or impact. Avoid pretending all risks become low after mitigation.

criteria.title: EU visibility under SMP-AGA rules
criteria.intent: To ensure EU funding is properly acknowledged across all communication outputs under SMP-AGA visibility rules.
criteria.elements: All materials must display: the "Funded by the European Union" emblem (NOT the Erasmus+ logo, NOT the EISMEA logo as a substitute); the standard disclaimer ("Views and opinions expressed are however those of the author(s) only and do not necessarily reflect those of the European Union or the European Innovation Council and SMEs Executive Agency (EISMEA). Neither the European Union nor the granting authority can be held responsible for them."); the FSTP must be communicated to SMEs as "co-funded by the European Union" (not as direct EU funding); visibility applies to all outputs: digital, printed, events, training materials, FSTP communications.
criteria.example_weak: We will mention EU support.
criteria.example_strong: All project outputs display the 'Funded by the European Union' emblem and the standard disclaimer per SMP-AGA Article 17; FSTP communications to SMEs are labelled 'co-funded by the European Union'; visibility is monitored quarterly across all partners' materials.
criteria.avoid: NEVER reference the Erasmus+ logo (this is SMP-COSME). Avoid inconsistent branding. Avoid presenting FSTP as direct EU funding.

criteria.title: Financial responsibility and dual co-financing structure
criteria.intent: To ensure financial responsibilities are clearly distributed and reflect the dual co-financing rate of SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME.
criteria.elements: The answer must explain: the coordinator's responsibility for overall financial coordination and EISMEA compliance; each partner's responsibility for managing its allocation under SMP-AGA rules; the dual co-financing rate (100% for FSTP / 90% for all other categories); the 10% co-financing contribution from the consortium on non-FSTP categories, distributed proportionally; the pre-financing arrangement (70% upfront) and balance payment.
criteria.example_weak: Stating that all partners manage the budget equally without structure.
criteria.example_strong: The coordinator ensures overall financial coordination and EISMEA reporting; each partner manages its allocation under SMP-AGA rules; the FSTP budget line (~27%) benefits from 100% co-financing while other categories (Personnel, Travel, Subcontracting, Other goods/services, Indirect costs) are co-financed at 90%, with the remaining 10% contributed proportionally by partners; the 70% pre-financing covers the early implementation phase including the FSTP open call.
criteria.avoid: Avoid unclear financial roles. Avoid missing the dual co-financing rate. NEVER reference EACEA.

criteria.title: Continuous financial monitoring and FSTP-specific control
criteria.intent: To ensure financial management is continuously monitored, with specific controls for the FSTP cost category.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe: quarterly financial reports from partners to the coordinator; integration of financial monitoring with operational tracking; specific controls for FSTP (lump-sum payment milestones, recipient declarations on double funding, audit-ready documentation for each FSTP grant); deviation thresholds and corrective procedures.
criteria.example_weak: Stating monitoring will be done regularly without explanation.
criteria.example_strong: Quarterly financial reports are consolidated by the coordinator; FSTP payments are released in lump sums against milestone achievement by each SME recipient, with audit-ready documentation and signed double-funding declarations at the start and end of each FSTP sub-grant; deviations above 10% trigger corrective procedures within 30 days.
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague monitoring statements. Avoid omitting FSTP-specific controls.

criteria.title: Continuous dissemination across the project lifecycle
criteria.intent: To ensure dissemination is continuous and strategic over time.
criteria.elements: Pre-implementation (M1-M6): awareness campaigns to attract SMEs and stakeholders; project launch event; first video. In-implementation (M6-M30): continuous updates on FSTP, route co-design, SME case studies; quarterly multiplier events; partner channel activations. Post-implementation (M30-M36 and beyond): final video, replicability toolkit publication on EU Tourism Platform, scaling webinars, integration into national tourism strategies, post-project entity communication.
criteria.example_weak: We will disseminate results at the end.
criteria.example_strong: Dissemination starts at project launch (M1) with the first video and FSTP awareness campaign, continues with quarterly updates and multiplier events, and culminates at M30-M36 with the final video, the toolkit publication on the EU Tourism Platform, scaling webinars, and handover to the post-project entity (EGTC) which continues communication through its own channels.
criteria.avoid: Avoid end-of-project-only dissemination. Avoid unclear timing.

criteria.title: Long-term systemic transformation beyond project duration
criteria.intent: To ensure the project generates lasting impact in the route ecosystem and the wider European tourism sector beyond the funding period.
criteria.elements: The answer must explain: continued use of the route, the digital platform, the FSTP-supported improvements, and the post-project governance entity (EGTC, AEIE, association); persistence of cross-border cooperation between partners; integration of the route into national tourism strategies; transferability of the toolkit to other European territories; potential for follow-up funding through Interreg, LIFE, future SMP-COSME, etc.
criteria.example_weak: The project will have long-term impact.
criteria.example_strong: Post-M36, the route is governed by an EGTC constituted in M30, sustained financially through SME fees, booking commissions and tour operator partnerships; partners maintain cross-border cooperation through the EGTC's structures; the toolkit is published on the EU Tourism Platform and replicated in 10+ European territories through peer-learning webinars; follow-up funding pathways identified (Interreg POCTEFA, LIFE, next SMP-COSME windows).
criteria.avoid: Avoid generic sustainability claims. Avoid abstract statements not linked to real mechanisms (jurídico, financial, organisational).

criteria.title: Practical and FSTP-aware mitigation measures
criteria.intent: To ensure each risk has realistic, operational mitigation strategies adapted to the route ecosystem and FSTP context.
criteria.elements: Mitigation measures must be concrete and adapted to tourism realities: flexible scheduling outside peak season, hybrid participation formats, multilingual SME-facing materials, diversified SME outreach channels (chambers, DMOs, associations), early FSTP open call (M9-M12 to allow time), tiered FSTP amounts (7k/10k/15k), contingency planning. For FSTP-specific risks: independent evaluation committee, conflict of interest declarations, mandatory double-funding declarations, milestone-based payments.
criteria.example_weak: The risk will be managed by partners.
criteria.example_strong: Mitigation: FSTP open call launched at M9 (before high season), with at least 3-month open period; SME outreach through 200+ identified candidates across 3 channels (DMOs, Chambers, associations); tiered FSTP amounts (7k/10k/15k) accommodating different SME absorption capacities; multilingual materials (ES/FR/EU); independent evaluation committee with published criteria and conflict of interest declarations.
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague mitigation statements. Avoid ignoring FSTP good practice requirements.

criteria.title: Monitoring, evaluation and EU Tourism Dashboard alignment
criteria.intent: To ensure project progress and results are measured through a structured indicator system compatible with the EU Tourism Dashboard.
criteria.elements: The answer must define quantitative indicators (number of SMEs receiving FSTP, % SMEs adopting digital tools, jobs created/maintained, t CO₂ saved, number of new visitors to under-visited territories, reduction in seasonality, number of stakeholders engaged) and qualitative indicators (SME satisfaction surveys, stakeholder engagement assessments, governance maturity self-assessment), with units, baseline and target values, mapped to EU Tourism Dashboard categories. Data collection methods must be described: FSTP recipient reports, SME surveys (at FSTP start, mid, end), tourism flow data from DMOs, EUROSTAT alignment, EC Communication Indicators for WP3.
criteria.example_weak: Stating that the project will reach "many SMEs" without specifying numbers or measurement methods.
criteria.example_strong: Indicator: number of tourism SMEs receiving FSTP and reporting adoption of at least one digital tool (unit: number of SMEs; baseline: 0; target: 25 by M30; mapped to EU Tourism Dashboard category 'Digital'). Data collection: FSTP recipient mandatory reports at M+6, M+12 and project end.
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague or non-measurable indicators. Avoid omitting baseline/target. Avoid disconnection from EU Tourism Dashboard.

criteria.title: Synergies and EU follow-up funding pathways
criteria.intent: To show how the project connects to other EU initiatives and creates continuity beyond one project cycle.
criteria.elements: The answer must identify concrete follow-up funding pathways: Interreg POCTEFA (cross-border continuation), LIFE (sustainability and biodiversity extensions), next SMP-COSME windows (replicability rollouts), Horizon Europe (research dimensions), LEADER (rural development extensions), ERDF (regional infrastructure), Creative Europe (cultural dimensions). It must explain how results are positioned for transfer to these programmes, avoiding double funding.
criteria.example_weak: We will collaborate with other projects.
criteria.example_strong: Follow-up pathways: a Phase 2 application to Interreg POCTEFA at M30 for cross-border scaling; a LIFE application focusing on the biodiversity and pastoral landscape dimension; engagement with future SMP-COSME windows for replicability rollouts; LEADER cooperation with rural development groups in both countries. Strict avoidance of double funding through coordinated funding plan.
criteria.avoid: Avoid generic synergy statements. Avoid isolated project thinking. NEVER imply double funding.

criteria.title: Effective cooperation and internal coordination
criteria.intent: To ensure the team operates as a coordinated system with clear communication and structured collaboration.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe collaboration mechanisms: monthly online coordination calls per WP, biannual in-person meetings, shared collaboration platform (project management tool, document repository), reporting templates, language protocols (English as working language, with translation support for SME-facing materials).
criteria.example_weak: Stating partners will collaborate without explaining how.
criteria.example_strong: Each WP holds monthly online coordination calls; the full Steering Committee meets biannually in-person, rotating between Spanish and French partner sites; a shared platform (e.g. SharePoint/Teams) hosts all working documents; English is the consortium working language with French/Spanish/Basque/Euskara used for SME-facing materials.
criteria.avoid: Avoid generic descriptions. Avoid omitting language coordination for transnational teams.

criteria.title: Proven capacity in tourism, SME support and FSTP delivery
criteria.intent: To ensure each partner has the demonstrated capacity, resources and experience to fulfil its role.
criteria.elements: The answer must highlight each partner's relevant track record: DMOs' experience in tourism strategy, route management, EU-funded projects (Interreg, COSME, LIFE); BSOs' experience in SME support, FSTP design and delivery (mandatory for BSOs leading WP2.2), capacity building, cross-border cooperation. PIC validation status must be confirmed.
criteria.example_weak: Describing partners in generic terms without proven experience.
criteria.example_strong: The coordinator has participated in 160+ EU-funded projects (25 as lead, 139 as partner), with €12.7 M cumulative EU grant volume; the French Chamber has delivered FSTP in 2 prior COSME-funded projects; both DMOs have ongoing Interreg POCTEFA experience covering the project's cross-border territory; PIC validation in progress for all 6 partners.
criteria.avoid: Avoid generic experience claims. Avoid omitting FSTP-specific experience for the BSOs leading WP2.2. Avoid omitting PIC status.

criteria.title: Relevance to SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME and European added value
criteria.intent: To show that the project is precisely aligned with this specific call and that it creates real EU-level impact through a route-based intervention.
criteria.elements: The answer must connect the project explicitly to the call's themes and priorities: thematic/geographical route creation, simultaneous correction of over-tourism and under-tourism, diversification of offer, regenerative and authentic tourism, valorisation of tangible and intangible heritage, twin transition of tourism SMEs, support for territorial cohesion. Then it must articulate European added value: shared methodologies developed and tested across countries, cross-country peer learning, scalability of route models, harmonisation with EU policy priorities, transferability of the resulting toolkit.
criteria.example_weak: The project is European because partners are from different countries.
criteria.example_strong: By co-designing and testing a transnational tourism route model with shared support mechanisms for SMEs across two European destinations, the project enables transfer and scaling of competitive and sustainable practices, directly contributing to the SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME objectives of route ecosystem strengthening, twin transition acceleration, and balanced territorial development.
criteria.avoid: Avoid listing EU priorities without explaining how the project contributes. Avoid assuming EU added value automatically. Avoid generic references to "European cooperation" without operational substance.

criteria.title: Transnational dimension and European added value
criteria.intent: To demonstrate that working at transnational level produces stronger results than isolated national actions.
criteria.elements: The answer must explain how cross-border cooperation between DMOs and BSOs from at least two countries improves the quality, transferability and scalability of the route ecosystem. It must show that the route model can be tested in diverse territorial contexts (e.g. one Spanish and one French side), enabling robust validation and adaptation. It must articulate transferability potential to other European route initiatives and aggregation of impact at EU level.
criteria.example_weak: Saying the project is European because partners are from different countries.
criteria.example_strong: By co-designing and operating a transnational route ecosystem across Spanish and French territories with comparable structural challenges (rural depopulation, twin transition gaps, seasonal coastal over-tourism), the project tests a route model in two distinct national policy contexts, producing a validated and transferable framework applicable to other European cross-border destinations.
criteria.avoid: Avoid defining EU added value only by the number of countries involved. Avoid vague statements about cooperation.

criteria.title: Integrated planning, monitoring and control
criteria.intent: To ensure implementation is continuously tracked and adjusted.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe: detailed Gantt with milestones M1-M36; KPI tracking aligned with EU Tourism Dashboard categories; monthly progress reports from WP leaders to Coordinator; quarterly Steering Committee reviews; integration with SMP reporting cycle (interim report at M18, final at M36+60 days); corrective action protocols when KPIs deviate >10%.
criteria.example_weak: The project will follow a timeline.
criteria.example_strong: Implementation is planned through a Gantt with monthly milestones and KPIs aligned with the EU Tourism Dashboard; WP leaders submit monthly progress reports; the Steering Committee reviews quarterly; SMP reporting includes interim report at M18 and final at M36+60 days; KPI deviations >10% trigger corrective protocols within 30 days.
criteria.avoid: Avoid static planning without monitoring. Avoid omitting integration with SMP reporting.

criteria.title: Measurable indicators aligned with the EU Tourism Dashboard
criteria.intent: To ensure that each objective is measurable through a robust indicator system that allows monitoring, verification, and aggregation at European level.
criteria.elements: Each objective must include at least one indicator with: a clear name, a unit of measurement, a baseline value (zero if pre-project, or current value if pre-existing), and a target value at project end. Wherever possible, indicators must be compatible with the EU Tourism Dashboard categories (economic, environmental, social, digital, governance) so that project results can be aggregated at EU level. Indicators must cover quantitative dimensions (number of SMEs supported, % of SMEs adopting digital tools, number of new visitors to under-visited territories, reduction in seasonality, jobs created/maintained, t CO₂ saved) and qualitative dimensions (SME satisfaction, stakeholder engagement, governance maturity).
criteria.example_weak: Defining objectives without measurable indicators, or using vague measurements such as "raise awareness" without specifying how it will be measured.
criteria.example_strong: Objective: improve SME capacity to adopt sustainable practices. Indicator: number of tourism SMEs along the route adopting at least one validated sustainable practice. Unit: number of SMEs. Baseline: 0. Target: 25 SMEs by M30. Aligned with EU Tourism Dashboard category 'Environmental Sustainability'.
criteria.avoid: Avoid non-measurable indicators. Avoid omitting baseline or target. Avoid indicators that are not aligned with project outputs. Avoid disconnection from the EU Tourism Dashboard framework.

criteria.title: Continuity, complementarity and transfer between WPs
criteria.intent: To ensure that all WPs are interconnected and contribute cumulatively to the route ecosystem outcomes.
criteria.elements: The answer must explain how outputs from one WP feed into the next: WP1 deliverables (route map, ecosystem mapping, KPI baseline, business plan) feed WP2 (FSTP criteria, SME engagement); WP2 results (SME pilots, capacity building outputs) feed WP3 (communication, case studies) and WP4 (best practices); WP3 and WP4 produce the toolkit and replicability outputs published on the EU Tourism Platform.
criteria.example_weak: Stating that WPs are connected without showing how.
criteria.example_strong: Tools developed in WP1 (ecosystem map, KPI framework, route business plan) define FSTP criteria in WP2.2; pilot results from FSTP recipients feed WP3 case studies and WP4 best practices, which are published in the EU Tourism Platform and shared through peer-learning webinars.
criteria.avoid: Avoid assumed coherence. Avoid failing to articulate the flow of outputs across WPs.

criteria.title: WP3 — Communication, Promotion and Branding of the Route Ecosystem
criteria.intent: WP3 gives the route ecosystem its public face: brand, narrative, digital infrastructure, multilingual content, and integration with the Destination Europe framework. It MUST include the mandatory deliverables of the call: minimum 2 short videos (1-2 min each) and participation in the mandatory Brussels event (2 days, 5 people).
criteria.elements: WP3 typically runs M3-M36 (continuous). Tasks: T3.1 communication strategy with EC Communication Indicators framework; T3.2 visual identity and brand book; T3.3 digital platform (multilingual website + app with mapping, booking integration or federation, AR for intangible heritage, public KPI dashboard); T3.4 multilingual content production (ES, FR, EN, EU); T3.5 social media strategy (LinkedIn for institutional, Instagram and TikTok for traveller audiences); T3.6 production of the 2 mandatory videos (one at M3, one at M30); T3.7 multiplier events (3 per country); T3.8 mandatory Brussels event participation (2 days, 5 people); T3.9 alignment with Destination Europe framework, Visit Europe portal, and national portals; T3.10 SME branding kits for FSTP recipients. Deliverables WP3: D3.1 Communication strategy; D3.2 Brand book and identity; D3.3 Multilingual digital platform; D3.4 Content library (multilingual); D3.5 Social media campaigns; D3.6 Video 1 (M3) and Video 2 (M30); D3.7 Multiplier events reports (6 total); D3.8 Brussels event participation report; D3.9 Destination Europe alignment note; D3.10 SME branding kits. Milestones: Brand launched (M6); Platform online (M12); Video 1 published (M3); Video 2 published (M30); Brussels event attended (year 2 or 3 per EISMEA convocation).
criteria.example_weak: WP3 limited to social media and a basic website, without alignment with Destination Europe framework, without mandatory videos and Brussels event, without proper EU visibility under SMP-AGA rules.
criteria.example_strong: WP3 with full multilingual digital platform integrated with Destination Europe and Visit Europe, the 2 mandatory videos, the mandatory Brussels event participation, EC Communication Indicators tracking, SME branding kits, and SMP-AGA-compliant EU visibility on all materials.
criteria.avoid: NEVER use the Erasmus+ logo (this is SMP-COSME — use the "Funded by the European Union" emblem). NEVER omit the 2 mandatory videos. NEVER omit the Brussels event. NEVER skip alignment with Destination Europe framework. NEVER present FSTP recipients as receiving direct EU funding (use "co-funded by the EU").

criteria.title: WP4 — Production of Best Practices and Case Studies for Replicability
criteria.intent: WP4 capitalises on project experience and converts it into transferable knowledge for other European territories. The publication of results on the EU Tourism Platform is mandatory. WP4 ensures that the SUSTRAI route ecosystem becomes a replicable model rather than a one-off intervention.
criteria.elements: WP4 typically runs M18-M36. Tasks: T4.1 documentation of best practices and lessons learned through interviews with SMEs, DMOs, BSOs, stakeholders; T4.2 development of case studies on SMEs (FSTP recipients), governance models, digital solutions, intangible heritage valorisation; T4.3 production of the replicability toolkit (methodologies, templates, guides) under open-access license (Creative Commons BY 4.0 or equivalent); T4.4 peer-learning webinars (minimum 5) with DMOs, BSOs and tourism networks from other European territories; T4.5 mandatory publication of all results on the EU Tourism Platform; T4.6 production of policy briefs for policymakers (national tourism authorities, DG GROW, EISMEA); T4.7 design of the post-project replication strategy (which other European routes can be inspired); T4.8 archive of project materials in partner websites for ≥ 5 years per SMP-AGA Article 22. Deliverables WP4: D4.1 Best practices and lessons learned report; D4.2 Case studies portfolio (≥ 10 case studies); D4.3 Replicability toolkit (open-access); D4.4 Peer-learning webinars (recordings + reports); D4.5 EU Tourism Platform publications; D4.6 Policy briefs (≥ 2); D4.7 Replication strategy; D4.8 5-year archive plan. Milestones: First case studies published (M24); Toolkit v1 released (M30); EU Tourism Platform publication completed (M36); Policy briefs delivered (M36); All materials archived in partner websites by M36+30 days.
criteria.example_weak: WP4 limited to a final report without proper case studies, without EU Tourism Platform publication, without policy briefs, without open-access toolkit.
criteria.example_strong: WP4 producing a structured toolkit under open-access license, 10+ case studies, 5+ peer-learning webinars with other European tourism networks, mandatory publication on the EU Tourism Platform, 2+ policy briefs, and a clear replication strategy.
criteria.avoid: NEVER omit the mandatory EU Tourism Platform publication. NEVER skip the peer-learning component. NEVER present the toolkit as proprietary — it must be open-access. NEVER omit the 5-year archive requirement per SMP-AGA Article 22.

criteria.title: Integration of risk management into project governance
criteria.intent: To ensure risk management is integrated into the overall governance system and updated continuously.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe: a risk register reviewed at every quarterly Steering Committee meeting; assignment of mitigation actions to WP leaders; escalation procedures for high-impact risks; integration with the EISMEA reporting cycle (risk update in interim and final reports).
criteria.example_weak: Saying the risk register will exist without explaining how it is used.
criteria.example_strong: The risk register is reviewed quarterly by the Steering Committee; mitigation actions are assigned to WP leaders with deadlines; high-impact risks escalate to the coordinator and, if necessary, are flagged in the EISMEA interim and final reports.
criteria.avoid: Avoid treating risk management as a static document. Avoid disconnection from project governance.

criteria.title: Proactive risk management and SMP-compliant reporting
criteria.intent: To ensure proactive risk management and transparent reporting throughout the project.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe a risk register updated quarterly, mitigation protocols, integration with operational decisions, and the SMP reporting cycle: continuous reporting through the EISMEA Portal, interim financial and technical report at midterm, final report at M36 + 60 days, plus mandatory FSTP-specific reports.
criteria.example_weak: Saying risks will be managed without explaining how.
criteria.example_strong: A risk register identifying 10+ risks (operational, financial, reputational, FSTP-specific) is reviewed quarterly by the Steering Committee with mitigation actions assigned to WP leaders; reporting follows the SMP cycle with the interim report at M18, final report at M36+60 days, and FSTP-specific declarations as required by EISMEA.
criteria.avoid: Avoid ignoring risks. Avoid treating reporting as a formal obligation.

criteria.title: Alignment with call's specific objectives and EU policy framework
criteria.intent: To ensure that the needs analysis and objectives are visibly aligned with the specific objectives of SMP-COSME-2026-TOURSME and with the broader EU policy framework relevant to tourism.
criteria.elements: The answer must explicitly map project objectives to: (a) the specific objectives of the call (route creation, over/under-tourism rebalancing, diversification, regenerative tourism, intangible heritage, community well-being); (b) the relevant EU policy frameworks (European Agenda for Tourism 2030, Transition Pathway for Tourism, European Green Deal, Digital Compass 2030, Pact for Skills / PANTOUR, European Accessibility Act, Circular Economy Action Plan). The mapping must be operational — show how each objective contributes to specific priority areas.
criteria.example_weak: Listing EU strategies without explaining how the project contributes to each.
criteria.example_strong: Objective 1 (SME twin transition) directly contributes to the Transition Pathway for Tourism (topics 4, 6, 18) and to the Digital Decade targets for SME digital intensity. Objective 2 (under-tourism territories) aligns with the European Agenda for Tourism 2030 priority area on resilience and inclusion, and with the Circular Economy Action Plan.
criteria.avoid: Avoid superficial references to EU policies without operational mapping. Avoid using too many frameworks if they are not directly relevant. Avoid name-dropping without substance.

criteria.title: Alignment with EU policies and strategic priorities for tourism
criteria.intent: To ensure the project is clearly and meaningfully aligned with the broader EU policy framework for tourism, sustainability and SME competitiveness.
criteria.elements: The answer must demonstrate specific contribution to: European Agenda for Tourism 2030, Transition Pathway for Tourism (with concrete topic references), European Green Deal, Circular Economy Action Plan, Digital Compass 2030, Pact for Skills (Tourism partnership) and PANTOUR, European Accessibility Act, Sustainable and Smart Mobility Strategy, Sustainable Blue Economy (if coastal segments are involved), Farm to Fork Strategy (if gastronomy is involved). The contribution must be operational, not nominal.
criteria.example_weak: Listing EU strategies without explaining contribution.
criteria.example_strong: The project contributes to the Transition Pathway for Tourism topics 1 (sustainable mobility), 6 (SME digitalisation), 8 (tourism for territorial cohesion), 12 (cultural and creative industries cooperation), and 18 (skills), and to the European Agenda for Tourism 2030 priority areas 'Green transition' and 'Digital transition'.
criteria.avoid: Avoid superficial references. Avoid creating links that are not supported by the project content.

criteria.title: Measurable reach using EC Communication Indicators
criteria.intent: To ensure dissemination effectiveness is measurable using the EC Communication Indicators framework.
criteria.elements: Indicators must include: number of SMEs reached through dissemination activities; number of stakeholders engaged in events; social media reach and engagement rates; downloads of toolkits and policy briefs; multiplier event participation; EU Tourism Platform publication metrics; press coverage. All measured through the EC Communication Indicators framework. Monitoring tools: analytics, attendance lists, post-event surveys.
criteria.example_weak: We will measure communication impact.
criteria.example_strong: Reach measured through the EC Communication Indicators framework: 5,000+ unique website visitors/year, 50,000+ social media impressions, 2,000+ toolkit downloads, 300+ multiplier event participants, 1,000+ EU Tourism Platform views, and a quantified survey of intent-to-replicate among DMOs and BSOs reached.
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague reach indicators. Avoid omitting the EC Communication Indicators framework.

criteria.title: Embedded route ecosystem cooperation with associated stakeholders
criteria.intent: To ensure cooperation extends beyond formal partners into the wider route ecosystem.
criteria.elements: The answer must describe how partners coordinate through structured mechanisms (Steering Committee, WP leaders, joint coordination platform). It must also describe the role of associated entities (without funding) and Letters of Intent from territorial stakeholders (local authorities, parks, GAL/Leader networks, cultural associations) confirming engagement and uptake.
criteria.example_weak: Mentioning associated partners without defining their function.
criteria.example_strong: The 6 consortium partners coordinate through a Steering Committee, WP leaders and a shared platform; in addition, Letters of Intent are secured from 12 territorial stakeholders (5 ayuntamientos pasiegos, 2 mancomunidades, 2 natural parks, 3 cultural associations) confirming engagement and uptake of the route, anchoring the project in the real tourism governance of the territory.
criteria.avoid: Avoid mentioning associated entities without defining their function. Avoid describing cooperation without operational mechanisms.

criteria.title: Adequacy of human resources to project scope
criteria.intent: To ensure the project has sufficient and appropriately allocated human resources for a 36-month, 1.4 M€ project with FSTP delivery to 30 SMEs.
criteria.elements: The answer must show realistic FTE allocations across partners, alignment with the SMP budget category A, and capacity to deliver all WPs including the FSTP processing burden. It must indicate that key staff CVs are provided in the application annex.
criteria.example_weak: Presenting an unbalanced team with insufficient FSTP delivery capacity.
criteria.example_strong: Total project FTE: ~6 person-years across 36 months, distributed proportionally across partners according to WP leadership. CVs of key actors (project manager, FSTP managers at both BSOs, route designers at both DMOs) are provided in the application annex.
criteria.avoid: Avoid underestimating FSTP delivery effort. Avoid an unrealistic staffing structure.

criteria.title: Lean and proportionate governance
criteria.intent: To ensure the governance system is proportionate to the project size and avoids unnecessary administrative burden.
criteria.elements: The answer must explain that the governance is calibrated for 6 partners, 1.4 M€, 36 months: avoiding over-engineering, using digital tools to reduce burden, focusing in-person meetings on strategic moments (kick-off, midterm, final), and proportionate reporting cycles.
criteria.example_weak: Over-engineered or under-engineered governance.
criteria.example_strong: The governance is calibrated to 6 partners, 1.4 M€ and 36 months: 1 in-person meeting every 6 months (3 in total + kick-off + final), monthly online WP calls, lightweight monthly progress reports, and SMP-AGA-compliant reporting at midterm and final stages, minimising administrative burden while preserving full control.
criteria.avoid: Avoid disproportionate complexity. Avoid omitting digital tools for efficiency.

criteria.title: Suitability, feasibility and practical added value
criteria.intent: To ensure the methodology is realistic, feasible within 30-48 months, and produces practical added value for tourism SMEs and the route ecosystem.
criteria.elements: The answer must justify why the chosen methodology is appropriate for the target groups (tourism SMEs, DMOs, BSOs, territorial stakeholders), the objectives, and the available resources within the 30-48 month timeframe. It must show that the approach is balanced — combining co-design, FSTP, capacity building, communication and replicability — and not over-engineered. It must show that SMEs can directly apply results in real business contexts.
criteria.example_weak: Justifying feasibility with generic statements like "the methodology includes multiple activities".
criteria.example_strong: The methodology balances structural co-design with rapid SME engagement through FSTP, ensuring that within 36 months SMEs can both apply funded improvements (digitalisation, accessibility, sustainability) and integrate into the consolidated route brand, with replicability outputs ready for transfer by M36.
criteria.avoid: Avoid generic feasibility statements. Avoid designing overly complex methodologies. Avoid disconnection between the methodology and the route SME context.

criteria.title: Financial sustainability and revenue diversification
criteria.intent: To explain how the post-project entity sustains itself financially after EU funding ends.
criteria.elements: The answer must propose a credible revenue mix for the post-project entity: SME annual fees (tiered by size and services); commissions on bookings mediated through the SUSTRAI platform; partnerships with tour operators specialising in sustainable/regenerative tourism; sponsorships from territorial authorities; project-based income from future EU grants. A simplified financial projection must be sketched.
criteria.example_weak: We will look for future funding.
criteria.example_strong: Post-project entity financial model: 30 SME annual fees (tiered 200-1,000 €) ≈ 15,000 €/year; booking commissions (5% on platform-mediated bookings, projected 2,000 bookings/year @ 200 € average = 20,000 €/year); 3 tour operator partnerships @ 5,000 €/year = 15,000 €/year; territorial authority contributions ≈ 20,000 €/year; total ≈ 70,000 €/year covering basic operations (platform maintenance, brand, coordination), with project-based income from EU grants for development activities.
criteria.avoid: Avoid relying on a single funding source. Avoid vague financial plans. Avoid unrealistic sustainability assumptions.
